title: Notebook 19 Gloggur failure reduction umbrella
description: Umbrella P1 bug for the remaining Notebook 19 retrieval failures. Scope: (1) fix find positional scope parsing/recovery so rg-like trailing path operands are treated as scope and ambiguous misuse fails loudly; (2) fix code-query contraction and routing so identifier/declaration lookups stay definition-first while mixed behavior queries surface the correct implementation/test pair; (3) harden docs/workflow authority retrieval so parity/process/config queries collapse onto the correct authority pack instead of broad repo hits or brittle singleton winners. Completion is user-decided, not benchmark-gated. Create linked follow-ups for GF-4, GF-1/GF-2, and GF-3; preserve current find entrypoint and convenience behavior while making it more ripgrep-like.
design: Umbrella coordination issue for the Notebook 19 Gloggur failure-reduction family. Keep implementation in the linked GF child issues: bd-2tu3.1 owns find positional scope recovery, bd-2tu3.2 owns code-query routing recovery, and bd-2tu3.3 owns docs/workflow authority retrieval. Use this parent to preserve scope, sequencing, and final user acceptance only; do not attach unrelated router or parser work here.
acceptance_criteria:
- Linked GF child issues are either closed or explicitly deferred with current evidence
- Any remaining Notebook 19 retrieval failure is represented by a concrete child issue rather than vague parent prose
- Final closure summarizes which query contracts shipped and which examples remain out of scope
- Parent issue does not replace targeted regression tests in the child issues
notes: Audited during /audit-beads-quality on 2026-05-03. Existing description already names the three durable child scopes, but the parent lacked design, acceptance criteria, and estimate. Dependents bd-2tu3.1, bd-2tu3.2, and bd-2tu3.3 contain detailed implementation evidence and focused test nodes.

title: Reduce notebook 19 gloggur retrieval churn across find/router failure families
description:
This umbrella should no longer be treated as a vague benchmark note. The current repo has already decomposed most of notebook 19's gloggur-side failure family into concrete router/find contracts, and the remaining value here is cross-area coordination plus replay evidence rather than blank-slate search work.

The three main implementation surfaces are now explicit:
1. `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` owns the `find`-side positional scope, ripgrep-like query normalization, and structured recovery/failure codes.
2. `src/gloggur/search/router/hints.py`, `src/gloggur/search/router/engine.py`, and `src/gloggur/search/router/backends.py` own code-query classification, definition-first routing, mixed-query rebalancing, and verbatim-literal handling.
3. `src/gloggur/search/router/path_priors.py` plus the same router engine/backend layers own docs/workflow authority routing and authority-pack narrowing behavior.

Related issue records already carry the detailed subcontracts for these slices: `bd-2tu3.1`, `bd-2tu3.2`, `bd-2tu3.3`, `bd-sq4z`, and `bd-j5b`. What remains at the umbrella level is to keep those slices from drifting apart when combined notebook-style command replays hit path-scope parsing, router classification, and authority-pack decisions in the same session. Full benchmark reruns are still useful confidence work, but they are follow-up validation rather than the only closure gate.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`
  - owns `find` positional-scope parsing, pathlike-token detection, structured missing/ambiguous scope failures, and additive recovery warnings.
  - also shapes the CLI JSON/debug payloads consumed during notebook-style replay.
- `src/gloggur/search/router/hints.py`
  - classifies query kind/domain for code vs docs/workflow requests and extracts literal/symbol/path hints.
- `src/gloggur/search/router/engine.py`
  - merges query-compat parsing, exact/symbol/semantic backend decisions, definition-first decisiveness, and narrowing behavior.
- `src/gloggur/search/router/backends.py`
  - owns exact-backend ranking adjustments, symbol-backend behavior, authority-aware lexical scoring, and literal-first exact handling.
- `src/gloggur/search/router/path_priors.py`
  - defines docs/workflow authority membership and domain-specific path priors.
- Regression coverage is already concentrated in `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py`, `tests/unit/test_search_router.py`, and the relevant `tests/integration/test_cli.py` router/find nodes.

Recommended scope framing for this umbrella:
- coordination issue across multiple already-shipped subcontracts;
- benchmark-family replay and drift detection issue, not a request to re-implement the router from scratch;
- child issues should keep the detailed per-slice contracts, while this record tracks the combined notebook-family outcome.
acceptance_criteria:
- The notebook-19 failure family remains decomposed into concrete `find`, code-query, and docs/workflow authority subcontracts rather than regressing back into one vague umbrella description.
- Representative direct command replays for those three families continue to match the shipped child-contract behavior.
- Changes touching `find` normalization, router query classification, or authority routing keep the targeted regression tests updated together so notebook-style combined behavior does not silently drift.
- Full benchmark reruns remain optional confidence evidence and are not the sole blocker for preserving the umbrella contract, but any future replay evidence should map failures back to the correct child surface instead of reopening the umbrella generically.
notes:
Current verification evidence gathered 2026-03-26:
- The detailed subcontracts already live in enriched child/follow-up issues:
  - `bd-2tu3.1` for `find` positional scope recovery/hardening
  - `bd-2tu3.2` for code query contraction / definition-first routing
  - `bd-2tu3.3` for docs/workflow authority retrieval
  - `bd-sq4z` / `bd-j5b` for adjacent `find` contract hardening around grep-like literals and byte offsets
- Current code ownership aligns with that decomposition:
  - `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` for `find` parsing and CLI payloads
  - `src/gloggur/search/router/hints.py`, `engine.py`, `backends.py`, and `path_priors.py` for routing and authority behavior
- Existing focused tests already cover the main slices independently, including query-kind/domain classification and the `find` path-scope contract.
- This issue is therefore best read as a coordination/replay umbrella for notebook 19 rather than as a low-detail request to add one more generic search fix.
- The main remaining risk is cross-surface drift: each child slice can stay green in isolation while notebook-style combined replay still regresses if path normalization, router classification, and authority-pack narrowing change independently.

title: Restore definition-first ranking for code symbol lookups
description: Current code shows this regression was fixed by tightening both backend ordering and router decisiveness for code symbol lookups. `src/gloggur/search/router/backends.py` now classifies exact hits as `definition`, `import`, `reference`, `same_file_context`, or `auxiliary`, promotes definitions ahead of importers/docs/tests for code identifier and declaration queries, and filters exact/symbol declaration queries down to definitions when present. `src/gloggur/search/router/engine.py` then refuses to mark an exact or symbol result decisive unless the top selected hit is definition-like. That keeps import sites and wrapper/reference lines from winning `open_hit_1` on notebook-19-style queries even when their raw match score is high. The present issue is therefore the concrete definition-first ranking repair, not a generic benchmark-performance note.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `src/gloggur/search/router/backends.py`
  - `_definition_ordering_enabled(...)` and `_definition_only_exact_query(...)` gate the code-query-specific behavior.
  - `_classify_exact_match_role(...)`, `_finalize_exact_hit_roles(...)`, and `_final_exact_hits(...)` rank definitions ahead of import/reference/auxiliary lines and filter declaration queries to definitions.
  - symbol-backend results map `occurrence.kind == "def"` to definition-like hits and apply the same declaration filtering.
- `src/gloggur/search/router/engine.py`
  - `_top_hit_is_definition_like(...)` and `_decisive_non_semantic_result(...)` prevent exact/symbol strategies from being treated as decisive when the top hit is not a real definition.
- `tests/unit/test_search_exact_backend.py` and `tests/unit/test_search_router.py`
  - pin the exact/symbol ordering and the router’s non-decisive fallback behavior when imports or references float to the top.

Scope clarification:
- this is a ranking-contract fix for code symbol lookups;
- it does not redefine docs/workflow path ranking or generic mixed natural-language search behavior.
acceptance_criteria:
- Code identifier queries rank real definitions ahead of importers and reference-only lines when definitions are present.
- Code declaration queries return definition hits only when a definition is available.
- Exact and symbol strategies are not marked decisive for code identifier/declaration queries unless the top hit is definition-like.
- Notebook-19-style symbol lookups converge on the real definition path instead of import-site churn.
- Regression coverage pins both backend ordering and router decisiveness.
notes:
Current evidence on 2026-03-26:
- `src/gloggur/search/router/backends.py` still applies definition-aware ordering and declaration-only filtering for code queries.
- `src/gloggur/search/router/engine.py` still requires the top selected hit to be definition-like before exact/symbol results become decisive.
Focused verification on 2026-03-26:
- `tests/unit/test_search_exact_backend.py::test_run_exact_backend_code_identifier_queries_rank_definitions_before_importers`
- `tests/unit/test_search_exact_backend.py::test_run_symbol_backend_ranks_definitions_before_references_for_code_identifier_queries`
- `tests/unit/test_search_exact_backend.py::test_run_symbol_backend_code_declaration_queries_filter_to_definitions_when_present`
- `tests/unit/test_search_router.py::test_search_router_does_not_mark_code_identifier_results_decisive_when_top_hit_is_not_definition`
- `tests/unit/test_search_router.py::test_search_router_does_not_mark_code_declaration_results_decisive_when_top_hit_is_reference`
- included in the focused pytest batch -> `30 passed in 8.84s`

title: Fix static quality gates regression after find recovery patch
description:
Current code no longer shows the original Ruff failure, so this issue is best recorded as a verification-surface cleanup after `bd-dj46` rather than as a standalone search behavior change. `scripts/run_static_quality_gates.py` still defines the static verification contract across the verification control-plane files plus `src/gloggur`, and `tests/integration/test_run_static_quality_gates_harness.py::test_run_static_quality_gates_harness_succeeds_for_verification_surface` still requires all three stages (`ruff`, `mypy`, `black`) to pass.

This issue should therefore be read as a post-landing verification fix, not as another router-contract change.
design:

Important scope clarification:

Focused verification on 2026-03-26:

title: Reduce Notebook 19 search churn and harden find recovery
description: Current code shows this was a focused Notebook 19 hardening pass on the locator/search decision contract, not a generic ranking tune-up. The shipped behavior combines three pieces: `find_v1` now carries an additive machine-readable `decision.target` alongside `suggested_next_command`; locator mode avoids unnecessary semantic fallback work when lexical hits are already decisive or authority-scoped; and docs/workflow parity queries are treated as authority packs rather than over-eager singleton opens. In practice that means semantic assist can rerank lexical hits for `find --about`, but it does not fire when decisive lexical code hits or authority-scoped docs/workflow hits already answer the question, and ambiguous authority packs stay multi-hit instead of collapsing to `open_hit_1`. The current issue is therefore the concrete Notebook-19 churn reduction around `find` recovery, semantic-fallback gating, and docs/workflow intent handling.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`
  - `_build_find_decision_target(...)` adds machine-readable `decision.target` to the `find_v1` payload.
  - `_build_find_suggested_next_command(...)` keeps the narrowing loop explicit without replacing the additive target payload.
- `src/gloggur/search/router/engine.py`
  - bounded-about semantic assist now checks decisive lexical hits, auxiliary-only lexical packs, and authority-scoped hits before deciding whether semantic rescue/reranking is needed.
- `src/gloggur/search/router/path_priors.py`
  - defines docs/workflow authority paths that feed the router’s authority-scoped logic.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py` and `tests/unit/test_search_router.py`
  - pin `decision.target`, decisive lexical skip behavior, authority-scoped workflow/doc behavior, and bounded semantic reranking.
- `docs/state-diagrams/find-command-state.md`
  - documents `decision.target` as additive and explains the degraded semantic-fallback case when lexical hits survive.

Scope clarification:
- this issue is narrower than the whole `find` feature;
- it is the churn-reduction pass that stabilized direct-open vs narrow-by-path behavior for Notebook-19-style queries.
acceptance_criteria:
- `find_v1` includes additive machine-readable `decision.target` for direct open or scope-narrowing actions.
- Locator semantic assist skips semantic fallback when lexical hits are already decisive or authority-scoped.
- Docs/workflow authority packs remain non-singleton when multiple top authority hits should stay visible together.
- `find --about` can still rerank lexical candidates when bounded semantic assist is actually needed.
- Regression coverage pins the authority-path, semantic-fallback, and `decision.target` behavior together.
notes:
Current evidence on 2026-03-26:
- `docs/state-diagrams/find-command-state.md` still documents `decision.target` and explicitly notes the surviving-lexical-hit fallback behavior.
- `src/gloggur/search/router/path_priors.py` still defines docs/workflow authority paths that the router uses to suppress unnecessary semantic rescue.
Focused verification on 2026-03-26:
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_find_json_adds_assist_and_suggested_next_command`
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_find_json_includes_about_and_forwards_semantic_query`
- `tests/unit/test_search_router.py::test_search_router_locator_about_skips_semantic_on_decisive_lexical_hit`
- `tests/unit/test_search_router.py::test_search_router_locator_about_skips_semantic_on_authority_scoped_workflow_hits`
- `tests/unit/test_search_router.py::test_search_router_workflow_parity_queries_keep_authority_pack_non_singleton`
- included in the focused pytest batch -> `20 passed in 3.79s`
close_reason: Implemented Notebook 19 search churn hardening, find_v1 contract updates, docs, and focused verification.

title: Make find treat punctuation-heavy verbatim literals like rg -F
description:
Current code already implements locator-mode verbatim handling for malformed URL/path literals in `gloggur find`. Single-token slash-heavy or backslash-heavy queries such as `///example.com`, `http:///example.com`, `//example.com/path`, and `\\example.com\\path` are now treated as full literals instead of being degraded into fragment searches like `http`, `example.com`, or `path`, and they are no longer reinterpreted as missing trailing scope operands.

The shipped behavior spans the CLI, routing hints, exact backend, and auto-routing policy. `src/gloggur/cli/main.py:_looks_find_verbatim_literal_token(...)` keeps URL-like malformed literals in the query instead of feeding them into positional scope inference. `src/gloggur/search/router/hints.py:_detect_verbatim_literal(...)` marks the full token as `verbatim_literal`, and `src/gloggur/search/router/backends.py:run_exact_backend(...)` then sends that exact literal to ripgrep with `-F` and suppresses fragment fallback when the search intent is locator mode. `src/gloggur/search/router/engine.py` also removes symbol-backend noise for locator verbatim queries and only allows semantic fallback when the operator explicitly supplies bounded `--about` context.

This issue is therefore no longer an open bug-fix request. It is a record of the shipped contract and its boundaries. The current boundary is intentional: verbatim detection only applies to single-token non-whitespace literals with slash or backslash structure, while explicit grep-compat regex input can still opt out of the verbatim shortcut.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`
  - `_looks_find_verbatim_literal_token(...)` is the first guardrail. It returns true for single-token malformed URL-like literals containing a dot plus either `://`, `//`, or `\\`, which prevents `_probe_find_positional_scope_operand(...)` from classifying those tokens as inferred file/path scope.
  - This is what keeps `find http:///example.com --json` from failing early as a missing path-like operand.
- `src/gloggur/search/router/hints.py`
  - `_detect_verbatim_literal(...)` promotes any single-token literal containing `/` or `\\` and at least one alnum into `QueryHints.verbatim_literal`.
  - `_literal_first_query(...)` is a related generalization for punctuation-heavy single tokens such as `[5.2.x]`; that behavior is adjacent to this bug fix, but the malformed-URL/path cases specifically rely on `verbatim_literal`.
- `src/gloggur/search/router/backends.py`
  - `run_exact_backend(...)` uses the entire `verbatim_literal` as the primary exact-search pattern, forces `fixed_string=True`, and suppresses symbol/literal fragment fallback when locator mode is active.
  - This is the behavior that makes the query act like `rg -F` instead of a best-effort decomposition pass.
- `src/gloggur/search/router/engine.py`
  - locator verbatim queries drop the symbol backend entirely;
  - locator verbatim queries without bounded `--about` become exact-only and report `reason="verbatim_literal_locator"`;
  - bounded `--about` can still trigger semantic rescue after an exact miss or auxiliary-only lexical results;
  - explicit grep-compat regex input clears `verbatim_literal` so users can still opt into real regex semantics.
- `tests/unit/test_search_router.py`, `tests/unit/test_search_exact_backend.py`, `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py`, and `tests/integration/test_cli.py`
  - these tests collectively lock the CLI parsing, hint extraction, exact-backend fixed-string behavior, exact-only locator routing, and bounded semantic-rescue path.

Recommended scope interpretation for future follow-up:
- treat this issue as shipped behavior documentation rather than unfinished parser/search work;
- open a separate issue for any expansion beyond single-token slash/backslash literals, rather than widening this contract implicitly.
acceptance_criteria:
- `find` keeps single-token malformed URL/path literals such as `http:///example.com`, `///example.com`, `//example.com/path`, and `\\example.com\\path` in query text instead of reclassifying them as trailing scope paths.
- Locator-mode exact search uses the full literal with fixed-string semantics and does not fall back to fragment patterns after an exact miss.
- Locator-mode verbatim queries skip symbol-backend noise and return `no_match` on an exact miss unless the user explicitly supplies bounded `--about` context.
- Bounded `--about` can perform semantic rescue after an exact miss or auxiliary-only lexical results for the same literal query.
- The `find --json` surface remains on the slim `find_v1` contract while this behavior is added beneath it.
notes:
Current verification evidence gathered 2026-03-26:
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_find_url_like_single_token_remains_query_text` parametrizes `http:///example.com`, `//example.com/path`, and `\\example.com\\path` and confirms the routed `find` query remains unchanged rather than being consumed as positional scope.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py:_looks_find_verbatim_literal_token(...)` and `_probe_find_positional_scope_operand(...)` are the concrete CLI gates that implement that behavior.
- `tests/unit/test_search_router.py::test_extract_query_hints_detects_verbatim_literals` confirms `extract_query_hints(...)` records each malformed URL/path sample as `verbatim_literal`.
- `tests/unit/test_search_exact_backend.py::test_run_exact_backend_locator_verbatim_query_uses_full_literal_fixed_string` records a single ripgrep pattern equal to `http:///example.com` and asserts `-F` is present.
- `tests/unit/test_search_exact_backend.py::test_run_exact_backend_locator_verbatim_query_does_not_fallback_to_fragments_after_miss` confirms locator-mode exact search does not fall back from `//example.com/path` to fragments after a miss.
- `tests/unit/test_search_router.py::test_search_router_locator_verbatim_queries_skip_symbol_and_semantic_without_about` confirms locator-mode verbatim queries only run the exact backend and emit `reason="verbatim_literal_locator"`.
- `tests/unit/test_search_router.py::test_search_router_locator_verbatim_queries_allow_semantic_rescue_after_exact_miss` confirms bounded `--about` can still trigger `semantic_rescue` after an exact miss.
- `tests/integration/test_cli.py::test_cli_find_verbatim_literals_prioritize_exact_hits_over_fragment_noise` confirms `find` on `http:///example.com` and `///example.com` only returns the files that actually contain those full literals.
- `tests/integration/test_cli.py::test_cli_find_verbatim_literal_miss_returns_no_match_and_about_can_rescue` confirms `//example.com/path` and `\\example.com\\path` return exact-only `no_match` without `--about`, while `--about "normalize malformed slashed url host"` can rescue to the relevant Django helper file.
- Adjacent contract clue: `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py` also asserts `find --json` still reports `contract_version == "find_v1"`, so this fix does not require a contract bump.
- Focused verification run on 2026-03-26:
  - `.venv/bin/pytest -q -n0 tests/unit/test_search_router.py::test_extract_query_hints_marks_punctuation_heavy_single_token_literal_first tests/unit/test_search_router.py::test_extract_query_hints_detects_verbatim_literals tests/unit/test_search_router.py::test_search_router_grep_fixed_string_query_preserves_verbatim_literal tests/unit/test_search_router.py::test_search_router_locator_verbatim_queries_skip_symbol_and_semantic_without_about tests/unit/test_search_router.py::test_search_router_locator_verbatim_queries_allow_semantic_rescue_after_exact_miss tests/unit/test_search_exact_backend.py::test_run_exact_backend_locator_verbatim_query_uses_full_literal_fixed_string tests/unit/test_search_exact_backend.py::test_run_exact_backend_locator_verbatim_query_does_not_fallback_to_fragments_after_miss tests/unit/test_search_exact_backend.py::test_run_exact_backend_locator_literal_first_query_uses_fixed_string_without_fallback tests/integration/test_cli.py::test_cli_find_verbatim_literals_prioritize_exact_hits_over_fragment_noise tests/integration/test_cli.py::test_cli_find_verbatim_literal_miss_returns_no_match_and_about_can_rescue tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_find_url_like_single_token_remains_query_text`
  - result: `16 passed, 3 warnings in 3.24s`

title: Investigate Flask benchmark repo index failure and resume blockage during local verification
description:
This is still an open field-bug investigation, but the current code narrows the search space far more than the original narrative does. The observed benchmark-repo failure is a compound contract break across two separate surfaces: (1) file-level index work hitting `storage_error` and/or post-index `vector_metadata_mismatch`, and (2) interrupted staged-build discovery later refusing a narrowed rerun with `resume_blocked`.

`src/gloggur/indexer/indexer.py` currently turns exceptions during extract-checkpoint persistence and later staged-cache persistence into `storage_error`, while `validate_vector_metadata_consistency()` emits `vector_metadata_mismatch` when vector ids drift from cache chunks after pending-embed checkpoint rows are excluded. Separately, `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` refuses to start a fresh build when interrupted staged work exists but is not safely resumable; `_build_resume_candidate_from_manifest(...)` adds compatibility reasons such as `workspace_path_hash_mismatch` and `index_target_path_changed`, and `_build_index_resume_blocked_payload(...)` surfaces the fail-closed JSON contract.

Current deterministic coverage proves those pieces independently, but the repo still lacks a reproducible benchmark-repo fixture for the combined Flask failure. `tests/integration/test_cli.py` covers tampered vector metadata and transient storage errors; `tests/unit/test_resume_recovery.py` and `tests/integration/test_resume_recovery_integration.py` cover incompatible or manifestless staged builds and discard/resume behavior. `scripts/run_edge_bench.py` only validates a generated fixture or an explicitly provided repo path exists; it does not yet replay the real Flask benchmark failure that originally exposed this issue.

This issue should now be tracked as a targeted reproduction-and-isolation task: find the exact extract/persist/assertion site on the benchmark repo, keep `storage_error` and `vector_metadata_mismatch` distinct, and make sure follow-up verification reruns are not stranded behind an opaque resume blocker.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `src/gloggur/indexer/indexer.py`
  - extract-phase checkpoint persistence can currently fail into `storage_error` at `_persist_extract_checkpoint(...)`.
  - later embed/persist failures also classify as `storage_error` unless they are explicit provider errors.
  - `validate_vector_metadata_consistency()` is the source of the fail-closed `vector_metadata_mismatch` contract and already excludes pending-embed checkpoint paths.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`
  - `_build_resume_candidate_from_manifest(...)` computes staged-build compatibility and adds `workspace_path_hash_mismatch` / `index_target_path_changed` reason codes.
  - `_discover_resume_candidates(...)` distinguishes compatible candidates from blocked candidates before cleanup.
  - `_build_index_resume_blocked_payload(...)` / `_emit_index_resume_blocked(...)` produce the fail-closed JSON error contract used when a follow-up run would discard interrupted work.
- Existing deterministic regression anchors:
  - `tests/integration/test_cli.py` for `vector_metadata_mismatch` and transient `storage_error` behavior
  - `tests/unit/test_resume_recovery.py` for manifestless/incompatible staged-build discovery
  - `tests/integration/test_resume_recovery_integration.py` for actual resume-blocked/discard flows
- Harness gap worth calling out explicitly:
  - `scripts/run_edge_bench.py` and `tests/integration/test_run_edge_bench_harness.py` currently validate the generated fixture and missing-repo/threshold behavior, but do not replay the real benchmark repo that produced this issue.

Important scope clarification:
- this is not a generic "index is flaky" ticket;
- the open work is to isolate which failure family is primary on the real repo and then harden that boundary without collapsing distinct failure codes together;
- resume-block avoidance is part of the operator-verification workflow here, not a separate nice-to-have.
acceptance_criteria:
- There is a reproducible benchmark-repo or minimized fixture that triggers the same failure family observed on the Flask repo during local verification.
- The reproduction identifies whether the primary root cause is extract-checkpoint persistence, later staged-cache persistence, or post-index vector metadata validation, rather than only surfacing aggregate `storage_error` samples.
- Follow-up reruns after an interrupted failure either safely resume or fail with an actionable staged-build contract (`resume_candidate`, `resume_block_reason_codes`, discard/adopt guidance) instead of stranding verification behind an opaque blocker.
- `storage_error`, `vector_metadata_mismatch`, and `resume_blocked` remain distinct machine-readable failure families with stable guidance.
- Focused regression coverage is added around the isolated root cause or replay path so benchmark-repo verification is no longer the only proof.
notes:
Current verification evidence gathered 2026-03-26:
- `src/gloggur/indexer/indexer.py` currently classifies extract/persist exceptions as `storage_error` and vector/cache drift as `vector_metadata_mismatch`.
- `tests/integration/test_cli.py::test_cli_index_reports_vector_metadata_mismatch_on_tampered_vector_map` proves the current fail-closed vector-drift contract.
- `tests/integration/test_cli.py::test_cli_index_transient_vector_upsert_failure_is_not_sticky` proves a transient persist failure currently surfaces as `storage_error` without leaving sticky mismatch state.
- `tests/unit/test_resume_recovery.py::test_discover_resume_candidates_blocks_manifestless_or_incompatible_staged_work` proves the current resume-discovery block reasons are already explicit and machine-readable.
- `tests/integration/test_resume_recovery_integration.py::test_index_blocks_manifestless_staged_build_until_discard_is_explicit` proves the fail-closed `resume_blocked` contract and explicit discard escape hatch.
- There is still no repo-tracked Flask benchmark replay fixture or deterministic test that reproduces the combined field report end to end; that gap is the main reason this issue remains open instead of being split purely into already-covered subcontracts.

title: Recover from missing implicit trailing path guesses in find
description: Current `find` no longer hard-fails every missing implicit trailing path guess. `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` uses `_looks_find_pathlike(...)`, `_probe_find_positional_scope_operand(...)`, and `_resolve_find_query_parts(...)` to recover when the final token looks like a repo path but does not exist and earlier tokens still form a searchable query. That recovery is additive: `FindTrailingPathRecovery` emits warning code `find_trailing_path_missing_recovered`, text output adds a recovery note, and the search still runs. The fail-closed boundaries remain important: a path-only missing token still raises `find_trailing_path_missing`, explicit `--file` / `--path-prefix` disables implicit inference, and mixed existing/missing multi-token positional scope still raises `find_positional_scope_ambiguous`. URL-like dotted literals stay in the query instead of being misclassified as paths.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`
  - `_FIND_IMPLICIT_PATHLIKE_EXTENSIONS` defines the suffix-based implicit path heuristic.
  - `_looks_find_pathlike(...)` and `_looks_find_verbatim_literal_token(...)` split path guesses from dotted literal text.
  - `_resolve_find_scope_path(...)` probes cwd-relative and repo-relative candidates.
  - `_resolve_find_query_parts(...)` is the main recovery/fail-closed decision point.
  - `_render_find_trailing_path_recovery_note(...)` keeps the text-mode contract human-readable.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py`
  - pins recovered searches, text-mode recovery notes, unrecoverable path-only failures, and dotted non-path query text.
- `docs/ERROR_CODES.md`
  - keeps `find_trailing_path_missing` as the explicit hard-failure contract.

Scope clarification:
- recovery only applies to implicit trailing scope guesses;
- explicit scope flags still disable inference rather than silently recovering.
acceptance_criteria:
- A missing trailing path-like token is ignored with additive warning metadata when searchable query text remains.
- A path-only missing token still fails closed with `find_trailing_path_missing`.
- Mixed existing and missing trailing scope operands still fail closed with `find_positional_scope_ambiguous`.
- URL-like or malformed dotted literals remain part of the lexical query instead of being treated as paths.
- Plain-text output includes a recovery note when implicit-path recovery is used.
notes:
Current evidence on 2026-03-26:
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` still exposes `FIND_TRAILING_PATH_MISSING_RECOVERED_WARNING = "find_trailing_path_missing_recovered"`.
- `_resolve_find_query_parts(...)` still keeps recovery additive while preserving explicit error codes for unrecoverable cases.
Focused verification on 2026-03-26:
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_find_pathlike_trailing_token_missing_recovers_when_query_remains`
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_find_pathlike_trailing_token_missing_text_output_adds_recovery_note`
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_find_pathlike_single_token_missing_returns_structured_error`
- included in the focused pytest batch -> `20 passed, 3 warnings in 3.94s`

title: Make watch state persistence atomic under concurrent status/daemon access
description: The shipped fix makes watch-state persistence atomic across the daemon and CLI write paths instead of relying on direct truncate-and-write updates to the same JSON file. `src/gloggur/watch/service.py` now centralizes JSON persistence in `_atomic_write_json_file(...)`, and both `WatchService._write_state(...)` and `src/gloggur/cli/main.py::_write_watch_state(...)` merge existing state via `load_watch_state(...)` before replacing the target through a unique temp file in the same directory (`watch_state.json.tmp.<pid>.<thread>.<uuid>`). That closes the shared-temp / partial-write window that let `watch status --json` observe a half-written state file under concurrent daemon/status activity in CI. The current boundary is intentionally narrower than “ignore malformed state everywhere”: the service-side loader remains permissive for daemon progress writes, while `src/gloggur/cli/main.py::_read_watch_state_for_status(...)` still fails closed with a structured `io_failure` when the on-disk state file is genuinely malformed JSON.
design:
- `src/gloggur/watch/service.py::_atomic_write_json_file(...)` creates a unique temp path with pid, thread id, and `uuid4()`, writes JSON there, and atomically replaces the target while cleaning up the temp file on `OSError`.
- `src/gloggur/watch/service.py::WatchService._write_state(...)` routes daemon lifecycle updates, batch summaries, and stop/failure state writes through that helper.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py::_write_watch_state(...)` reuses the same helper for CLI-side watch state mutations during startup/teardown failure handling so both writer families share the same persistence contract.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py::_read_watch_state_for_status(...)` intentionally keeps a stricter read path than `load_watch_state(...)`: malformed JSON is surfaced as `operation = "read watch state file"` instead of being silently treated as empty state.
- Regression coverage lives in `tests/unit/test_watch_service.py::test_atomic_write_json_file_uses_unique_temp_paths_under_concurrent_writes`; malformed-file status behavior remains covered by `tests/unit/test_cli_watch.py::test_watch_status_json_reports_malformed_state_file`.
acceptance_criteria:
Concurrent writes to the same watch state path use distinct temp filenames and do not fail from temp-path collisions.
Daemon and CLI watch-state write helpers persist via atomic replace rather than direct truncate-and-write of the target JSON file.
`watch status --json` still exits non-zero with a structured `io_failure` / `read watch state file` payload when the state file contents are truly malformed.
Focused tests cover both the concurrent-write regression and the preserved malformed-state CLI boundary.
notes:
Landed in commit `1f5c403` (`fix(watch): make watch state persistence atomic`).
Original failure report referenced GitHub Actions run `23160299303` on the required Python 3.13 lane.
Verification: `./.venv/bin/pytest -q -n0 tests/unit/test_watch_service.py::test_atomic_write_json_file_uses_unique_temp_paths_under_concurrent_writes tests/unit/test_cli_watch.py::test_watch_status_json_reports_malformed_state_file` -> `2 passed in 2.16s`.

title: Restore cli/main.py coverage baseline for access and extract-worker branches
description:
Current repo no longer has the coverage regression, but this issue is best recorded as a targeted test-addition pass that kept the checked-in coverage contract unchanged. `scripts/check_coverage_baseline.py` still protects `bootstrap_launcher.py`, `cli/main.py`, `search/hybrid_search.py`, and `storage/metadata_store.py`, and `cli/main.py` is still one of the enforced protected modules.

The landing fix was additive test coverage rather than threshold churn: current file history shows `d4f7875` added focused tests in `tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py` and `tests/unit/test_cli_main_index_additional.py` for helper/command-family branches around guidance failures, coverage ingest/import behavior, and index-result aggregation, while the baseline script and harness contract stayed unchanged. This issue should therefore be treated as a coverage-recovery test pass, not as a baseline-lowering change.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `scripts/check_coverage_baseline.py`
  - `PROTECTED_MODULES` still includes `cli/main.py`.
  - the script still emits `coverage_threshold_exceeded` when overall or protected-module coverage drops below the checked-in baseline.
- `tests/unit/test_check_coverage_baseline.py` and `tests/integration/test_check_coverage_baseline_harness.py`
  - pin the baseline file shape, protected-module comparisons, and threshold-failure contract.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py`
  - now contains focused helper/command-family coverage for guidance failure paths and coverage ingest/import output/error handling.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main_index_additional.py`
  - covers index result aggregation and stage-checkpoint behavior that feeds the `cli/main.py` command-family surface.
- file history: `d4f7875` (`test(cli): restore cli main coverage baseline for helper branches`)
  - confirms the intended repair path was test coverage, not threshold changes.

Important scope clarification:
- this issue restored coverage by adding focused tests around helper branches;
- it did not relax the coverage gate or remove `cli/main.py` from the protected baseline set.
acceptance_criteria:
The checked-in coverage baseline remains authoritative and continues to protect `cli/main.py`.
Focused CLI tests cover the helper/command-family branches that previously left `cli/main.py` below the baseline.
`python scripts/check_coverage_baseline.py --format json` and its harness continue to fail closed on real regressions instead of accepting a lowered threshold.
The fix is test-only / verification-surface work rather than a baseline rewrite.
notes:
Landed in commit `d4f7875` (`test(cli): restore cli main coverage baseline for helper branches`).
Current evidence on 2026-03-26:
- `scripts/check_coverage_baseline.py` still protects `cli/main.py` via `PROTECTED_MODULES`.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py` now contains the added guidance and coverage import/ingest branch tests.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main_index_additional.py` contains additive index command-family coverage used to keep the baseline green.
Focused verification on 2026-03-26:
- `tests/integration/test_check_coverage_baseline_harness.py`
- selected `tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py::*` helper-branch nodes
- selected `tests/unit/test_cli_main_index_additional.py::*` nodes
- included in the focused pytest batch -> `29 passed, 3 warnings in 40.24s`

title: Restore static quality gate after large-catalog edge-worker changes
description:
Current repo state shows this closed issue was a narrow verification-only cleanup on top of the large-catalog edge-worker rollout from `bd-5n6v`, not a second functional change to indexing behavior. The live required-lane gate is `scripts/run_static_quality_gates.py`, which runs `ruff`, `mypy`, and `black` over the verification control-plane files plus `src/gloggur` for Ruff/Black. After the edge-worker refactor landed, that gate started failing on small style/lint regressions in the runtime package even though the build-edges execution contract itself was already functionally correct.

The shipped fix is visible in commit `961aaa1`: it performs a minimal cleanup in `src/gloggur/indexer/indexer.py` and `src/gloggur/graph/extractor.py`, plus a regenerated timestamp refresh in `docs/semantic-search-embedding-breakdown-base.md`, so the required static-quality lane goes back to green without changing the actual large-catalog edge-worker semantics. This issue is therefore best tracked as a required-lane Ruff/format recovery record attached to the edge-worker landing, not as a behavioral indexing follow-up.
design:
- Shared verification surface:
  - `scripts/run_static_quality_gates.py` defines the required static-gate plan and runs `ruff`, `mypy`, then `black` fail-closed.
  - `tests/integration/test_run_static_quality_gates_harness.py` locks the end-to-end JSON payload and target scope.
  - `tests/unit/test_run_static_quality_gates.py` locks blocked-stage and target-scope behavior.
- Edge-worker-specific touched files from the actual cleanup commit `961aaa1`:
  - `src/gloggur/indexer/indexer.py`: wraps the `use_primed_catalog` guard in a Ruff-compliant multiline condition inside `_extract_symbols_worker_main(...)`.
  - `src/gloggur/graph/extractor.py`: tightens wording and removes an unused local binding in the candidate-index overlay path.
  - `docs/semantic-search-embedding-breakdown-base.md`: regenerated artifact timestamp refresh from the hook flow.
- The functional edge-worker contract still lives elsewhere:
  - `tests/unit/test_indexer.py::test_build_edges_watchdog_uses_primed_catalog_payload`
  - `tests/unit/test_indexer.py::test_indexer_auto_large_catalog_falls_back_inline_and_reports_reason`
  Those tests are the real runtime guardrails; this issue only restored the verification lane around them.
acceptance_criteria:
- `scripts/run_static_quality_gates.py --format json` returns `ok=true` again after the edge-worker landing.
- `tests/integration/test_run_static_quality_gates_harness.py` passes on the branch containing the fix.
- The cleanup is limited to lint/format compliance in the touched runtime files and does not redefine the large-catalog build-edges contract.
- Existing edge-worker regression tests remain the source of truth for runtime behavior.
notes:
Landing commit: `961aaa1` (`fix(indexer): restore static quality gates after edge-worker refactor`).
Related feature landing: `f043c9e` (`feat(indexer): remove large-catalog edge IPC without changing indexed output`).
Current evidence gathered 2026-03-26:
- `scripts/run_static_quality_gates.py` still gates `src/gloggur` under Ruff/Black and stops later stages after the first failure.
- `tests/integration/test_run_static_quality_gates_harness.py` still asserts the stage order `ruff -> mypy -> black` and the inclusion of `src/gloggur` in target scope.
- `git show --stat --summary --patch 961aaa1` shows the fix was limited to `src/gloggur/indexer/indexer.py`, `src/gloggur/graph/extractor.py`, and the generated breakdown doc.
- `tests/unit/test_indexer.py::test_build_edges_watchdog_uses_primed_catalog_payload` and `tests/unit/test_indexer.py::test_indexer_auto_large_catalog_falls_back_inline_and_reports_reason` still lock the actual worker/inlining behavior that this bug did not intend to change.
close_reason: Completed; fixed Ruff regressions in src/gloggur introduced by the large-catalog edge-worker refactor and reverified static gates plus full pytest (1000 passed).

title: Eliminate large-catalog build_edges IPC without index drift
description:
Current repo state is a concrete execution-strategy contract around `build_edges`, not just “remove IPC.” `Indexer` still supports both extract-worker and inline execution, but large repository symbol catalogs now fail over deterministically instead of repeatedly shipping the full candidate symbol list through per-file worker IPC. `src/gloggur/indexer/indexer.py` fingerprints and primes the candidate catalog once per worker, publishes per-run diagnostics through `ExtractWorkerInfo`, and in `auto` mode forces `build_edges_mode = inline` with `reason_code = candidate_catalog_too_large` when the catalog crosses the inline threshold. The indexed symbols, chunks, and edges are expected to remain identical; only execution mode changes.

The shipped scope also includes resume/checkpoint and harness visibility. Regression coverage proves inline and forced-worker modes persist identical output, large catalogs in `auto` mode fall back inline with the published reason code, primed worker catalogs are reused, and interrupted inline large-catalog runs preserve checkpoints so resume only continues remaining files. The CLI now exposes additive `extract_worker` diagnostics, and the benchmark harness can pass through `GLOGGUR_BUILD_EDGES_WORKER_MODE` for reproducible verification.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `src/gloggur/indexer/indexer.py:402-436`: `_catalog_key()` and `prime_build_edges_catalog()` build and reuse one stable candidate-catalog fingerprint per worker lifetime.
- `src/gloggur/indexer/indexer.py:488-497` and `925-951`: `ExtractWorkerInfo` and `_extract_worker_info_for_run()` publish `prepare_file_mode`, `build_edges_mode`, `catalog_symbol_count`, and optional `reason_code`, including the large-catalog inline fallback.
- `src/gloggur/indexer/indexer.py:1245-1362` and `1540-1566`: `_build_edges_with_watchdog()` and repository indexing choose worker vs inline execution, reuse primed catalogs where possible, and keep inline fallback deterministic.
- `src/gloggur/config.py:155-169,453-454`: `build_edges_worker_mode` is a validated config/env surface with env override support through `GLOGGUR_BUILD_EDGES_WORKER_MODE`.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py:2923-2948,5703-5708,6098-6104`: additive `extract_worker` diagnostics are surfaced in index/find JSON payloads when available.
- `tests/unit/test_indexer.py:1787-2077`: coverage for persisted-output parity across inline/worker modes, large-catalog inline fallback, primed-catalog reuse, and resume checkpoint preservation.
- `scripts/run_edge_bench.py:97-113` plus `tests/unit/test_run_edge_bench.py` / `tests/integration/test_run_edge_bench_harness.py`: benchmark env passthrough for build-edges worker-mode experiments.

Recommended contract framing:
- execution mode is an optimization/control surface, not a semantics surface;
- large-catalog fallback must stay explicit and machine-readable;
- interrupted inline fallback runs must preserve staged checkpoints so resume semantics do not regress.
acceptance_criteria:
- Forced-worker and forced-inline `build_edges` execution produce identical persisted symbols, chunks, and edges.
- In `auto` mode, large candidate catalogs fall back to inline `build_edges` with `reason_code = candidate_catalog_too_large` and a reported `catalog_symbol_count`.
- Worker execution primes and reuses the candidate catalog instead of resending the full catalog for every file.
- Additive `extract_worker` diagnostics appear in CLI JSON payloads when available and include worker mode and reason metadata.
- Interrupted large-catalog inline runs preserve checkpoints so resume only processes remaining files.
- Benchmark harness/env plumbing allows explicit `GLOGGUR_BUILD_EDGES_WORKER_MODE` passthrough for reproducible verification.
notes:
Current verification evidence gathered 2026-03-26:
- `src/gloggur/indexer/indexer.py:402-436,488-497,925-951,1245-1362,1540-1566` shows the current catalog-priming, mode-selection, and fallback logic.
- `tests/unit/test_indexer.py:1787-1832` verifies worker and inline modes preserve identical persisted output.
- `tests/unit/test_indexer.py:1834-1859` verifies `auto` large-catalog fallback to inline with `reason_code = "candidate_catalog_too_large"`.
- `tests/unit/test_indexer.py:1862-2010` verifies primed-catalog reuse and the `use_primed_catalog = true` worker payload.
- `tests/unit/test_indexer.py:2013-2077` verifies interrupted inline large-catalog runs preserve checkpoints and resume skips already completed files.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py:2923-2948,5703-5708,6098-6104` shows additive `extract_worker` JSON payload plumbing.
- `tests/unit/test_run_edge_bench.py:90-96` and `tests/integration/test_run_edge_bench_harness.py:44-53` verify env passthrough for `GLOGGUR_BUILD_EDGES_WORKER_MODE`.
close_reason: Completed; implemented large-catalog build_edges worker fallback and primed-catalog reuse, added JSON diagnostics/env passthrough, and verified with targeted pytest suite (93 passed).

title: Fail loud when gloggur editable install points at a different checkout
description:
This issue is narrower and more concrete than the original “cache churn” sketch. The real failure mode is runtime provenance ambiguity across entrypoints: a repo-local editable environment can resolve `gloggur` from a different checkout or package root than the workspace the operator believes they are running, while current diagnostics primarily report cache/profile state rather than the actual imported module path.

Today the safest repo-local entrypoint is `scripts/gloggur`, which is bootstrap-aware and prepends the current repo import root to `PYTHONPATH` before launching. The riskier path is the installed console script `./.venv/bin/gloggur`, which is just a thin wrapper importing `gloggur.cli.main` directly. In editable installs, that import follows whatever package root the environment exposes, so a stale `.pth` or different worktree install can silently run another checkout's code while still reading this repo's `.gloggur-cache` and support directories.

Observed current state as of 2026-03-26:
- `./.venv/bin/gloggur` does not go through the repo bootstrap wrapper or any provenance guard; it imports `gloggur.cli.main` immediately.
- The current editable install metadata in this environment is `.venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/__editable__.gloggur-0.1.0.pth`, which points at `/Users/auzi/vinnustofa/gloggur/src` in the healthy case.
- Direct local probes show why provenance must be explicit rather than inferred from one path source:
  - interactive `.venv/bin/python -c 'import gloggur'` from repo root resolves `gloggur` from `/Users/auzi/vinnustofa/gloggur/gloggur/__init__.py` because `cwd` is on `sys.path`
  - simulated `./.venv/bin/gloggur` import order resolves `gloggur` from `/Users/auzi/vinnustofa/gloggur/src/gloggur/__init__.py`
- `status --json` reports cache/index-profile integrity and entrypoint metadata, but it does not report executable path, actual imported package root, or whether those paths match the current repo root.
- Support diagnostics and support-runtime traces similarly record `repo_root`, `cwd`, command args, and bootstrap/status artifacts, but not the actual import root or console-script executable path.
- When those provenance surfaces diverge across worktrees, the resulting symptoms look like repeated cache schema/index-profile rebuild churn with weak diagnostic attribution instead of an explicit environment mismatch failure.

The remaining work is therefore runtime provenance hardening: detect and surface which checkout/package root is actually executing, compare it with the expected repo root, and thread that information through normal status/support diagnostics so stale editable installs fail or warn loudly instead of masquerading as generic cache drift.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `./.venv/bin/gloggur` is currently a plain console script with:
  - shebang to the repo-local venv Python
  - `from gloggur.cli.main import main`
  - no bootstrap or provenance validation layer
- `scripts/gloggur` is the contrasting safer path:
  - computes `REPO_ROOT`
  - exports `PYTHONPATH=${REPO_ROOT}/src[:...]`
  - executes `python -m gloggur.bootstrap_launcher`
- `src/gloggur/bootstrap_launcher.py` already has partial provenance ingredients:
  - `_repo_root(...)` and `_import_root(...)` know the expected repo/import root for the wrapper path
  - `_prepend_pythonpath(...)` normalizes the import root onto `PYTHONPATH`
  - `build_ready_payload(...)` and `build_failure_payload(...)` expose `repo_root`, selected interpreter, probes, and `pythonpath`
  - but they do not report the actual resolved `gloggur` module file or compare it against the expected repo/import root
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py:_build_status_payload(...)` currently exposes cache/integrity fields such as `cache_dir`, `schema_version`, `entrypoint`, `expected_index_profile`, and `cached_index_profile`, but no executable/import-root provenance fields
- `src/gloggur/support.py` captures `config.redacted.json`, `bootstrap_status.json`, `status.json`, and `watch_status.json`, plus session metadata with `repo_root` and `cwd`, but it does not persist the active executable path or resolved imported package root
- `src/gloggur/support_runtime.py:CommandTraceSession` persists `argv`, `cwd`, and `repo_root` in runtime trace metadata, but again not executable/import-root provenance
- Docs and operator guidance already bias users toward `scripts/gloggur` (`AGENTS.md`, `docs/QUICKSTART.md`, `docs/AGENT_INTEGRATION.md`), which suggests the intended safe path exists conceptually even though the raw installed console script remains silent

Recommended implementation shape:
- add one shared provenance helper that captures at least:
  - current repo root
  - executable path / interpreter path
  - actual `gloggur` module file and normalized package root
  - expected import root for the current invocation mode
  - explicit booleans/reason codes for provenance match vs mismatch
- thread that helper into `status`, support bundle diagnostics, and support-runtime metadata
- decide failure policy carefully:
  - direct console-script mismatch should likely fail closed or at minimum emit a high-signal warning code
  - wrapper-based healthy launches should remain quiet
  - the policy should key off actual mismatch, not merely off using `.venv/bin/gloggur`

Important scope clarification:
- this is a runtime-entrypoint/provenance bug, not a cache-schema bug by itself
- the goal is not to forbid editable installs; it is to make the active checkout/package root explicit and mismatches loud
- the bootstrap wrapper already mitigates part of the risk, so the highest-value hardening is around direct console-script execution and diagnostic visibility
acceptance_criteria:
- The CLI can report deterministic runtime provenance including repo root, executable/interpreter path, actual imported `gloggur` package root, and a machine-readable match/mismatch verdict.
- `status --json` includes that provenance payload so cache/index-profile drift can be distinguished from environment-root drift.
- Support bundle diagnostics and support-runtime trace metadata include the same provenance fields, making stale editable installs diagnosable after the fact.
- Direct console-script execution from a mismatched editable install fails or warns loudly with a stable error/warning code instead of appearing as generic cache churn.
- Healthy repo-local wrapper execution (`scripts/gloggur ...`) remains supported without false-positive mismatch failures.
- Tests cover healthy provenance, mismatched import-root scenarios, and support/status payload serialization of the new fields.
notes:
Current verification evidence gathered 2026-03-26:
- Direct entrypoint probes:
  - `sed -n '1,80p' .venv/bin/gloggur` shows the installed console script is only:
    - shebang to `.venv/bin/python`
    - `from gloggur.cli.main import main`
    - `sys.exit(main())`
  - `sed -n '1,40p' .venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/__editable__.gloggur-0.1.0.pth` shows the editable install currently points at `/Users/auzi/vinnustofa/gloggur/src`
- Local healthy-environment probes:
  - interactive `.venv/bin/python` from repo root resolved `gloggur.__file__` to `/Users/auzi/vinnustofa/gloggur/gloggur/__init__.py` because `cwd` is on `sys.path`
  - a simulated `./.venv/bin/gloggur` import order (with `sys.path[0]` set to `.venv/bin` and repo-root `''` removed) resolved `gloggur.__file__` to `/Users/auzi/vinnustofa/gloggur/src/gloggur/__init__.py`
  - this proves the actual imported package root depends on invocation shape and is not derivable from `repo_root` alone
- Bootstrap wrapper state:
  - `scripts/gloggur` explicitly prepends `${REPO_ROOT}/src` to `PYTHONPATH` and executes `python -m gloggur.bootstrap_launcher`
  - `build_ready_payload(...)` currently emits `selected_interpreter`, `selected_candidate`, `repo_root`, and `pythonpath`, but no actual imported module path
  - direct probe of `build_ready_payload(...)` for `status --json` produced only `repo_root`, `pythonpath`, probe data, interpreter, and bootstrap-state fields
- Status/support omission evidence:
  - live `status --json` top-level keys include cache/integrity fields such as `cache_dir`, `schema_version`, `entrypoint`, `expected_index_profile`, and `cached_index_profile`, but no executable/import-root provenance fields
  - `src/gloggur/support.py` support-session and diagnostics payloads record `repo_root`, `cwd`, `config_path`, bootstrap status, status payload, and watch payload only
  - `src/gloggur/support_runtime.py:CommandTraceSession` metadata records `command_name`, `argv`, `cwd`, `repo_root`, pid, stage, and build state, but not executable/import-root provenance
- Focused regression coverage already present around adjacent surfaces:
  - `tests/unit/test_bootstrap_launcher.py::test_failure_payload_contains_stable_fields`
  - `tests/unit/test_bootstrap_launcher.py::test_main_dry_run_emits_ready_payload_without_exec`
  - `tests/unit/test_support_runtime.py::test_command_trace_session_moves_completed_command_to_recent`
  - `tests/integration/test_support_cli.py::test_support_collect_manual_snapshot_includes_runtime_logs`
- Direct verification run on 2026-03-26:
  - `.venv/bin/pytest -q -n0 tests/unit/test_bootstrap_launcher.py::test_failure_payload_contains_stable_fields tests/unit/test_bootstrap_launcher.py::test_main_dry_run_emits_ready_payload_without_exec tests/unit/test_support_runtime.py::test_command_trace_session_moves_completed_command_to_recent tests/integration/test_support_cli.py::test_support_collect_manual_snapshot_includes_runtime_logs`
  - result: `4 passed, 3 warnings in 2.55s`
- Gap between the original low-signal wording and current code:
  - the wrapper path already has partial provenance context (`repo_root`, `pythonpath`, selected interpreter)
  - the missing piece is actual import-root verification and propagation into normal diagnostics
  - the primary silent-risk surface is direct installed-console-script execution, not the documented `scripts/gloggur` workflow

title: Persist access grant marker on ready init/access success
description:
Current code already ships this fix. Before the bug fix, `gloggur init` and `gloggur access grant` could report success for an already-ready repository while still leaving `.gloggur/access_grants.json` absent if `.gloggur` and `.gloggur-cache` already existed and no actual access mutations were needed. That produced a false-ready state: access planning said the repo was ready, but the persisted grant marker that downstream tooling and operators expect was missing.

The shipped behavior now treats the marker file as part of the success contract, not just a side effect of mutating grant application. `apply_access_grant(...)` still persists state for non-ready plans, but already-ready plans now flow through `persist_ready_access_grant_state(...)`, and both `init` and `access grant` call that helper whenever `access_ready=true` but the marker file may be missing.

Observed current state as of 2026-03-26:
- Already-ready repos with a missing marker are repaired on successful `init` and `access grant` paths rather than being left in a silent partial state.
- The fast path intentionally preserves the public payload shape: `access_ready` is `true`, `access_result` remains `null`, and no synthetic `applied_actions` payload is introduced when no access mutation was required.
- The repaired marker file uses the same `schema_version = "1"` persisted grant-state payload shape as the mutating `apply_access_grant(...)` path.
- The shell wrapper inherits the same behavior because it delegates directly to the CLI `access grant` command.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `src/gloggur/access.py`
  - `apply_access_grant(...)` remains the mutating path for non-ready plans and writes `.gloggur/access_grants.json` through `_write_access_grant_result(...)`.
  - `persist_ready_access_grant_state(...)` is the narrow bug-fix path for this issue: it requires `plan.access_ready`, builds an `AccessGrantResult` with empty `applied_actions` / `blocked_paths` / `manual_actions`, sets `access_ready=true`, and writes the same persisted state-file schema without touching repo permissions.
  - `_write_access_grant_result(...)` centralizes the file format, parent-dir creation, and I/O failure wrapping so the ready fast path and mutating path share one persistence contract.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`
  - `_run_access_grant_flow(...)` now short-circuits already-ready plans by calling `persist_ready_access_grant_state(access_plan)` and returning `None` before prompt / `--yes` logic, which preserves the existing `access_result = null` CLI contract on the ready fast path.
  - `_init_impl(...)` has a second explicit repair path for `--no-grant-access`: when the plan is already ready and the user skips the grant flow, it still calls `persist_ready_access_grant_state(...)` so `init` cannot leave a missing marker behind.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py`
  - locks the four regression cases that matter here: ready repo marker repair through `init --yes`, `init --no-grant-access`, `access grant --yes`, and `access grant` without `--yes` in JSON mode.
- `tests/unit/test_access.py`
  - locks the underlying state-file schema and existence contract for the mutating grant path.
- `tests/integration/test_access_wrapper.py`
  - confirms the shell wrapper preserves the same marker-persistence outcome via the CLI.

Important scope clarification:
- this issue is not about broadening access permissions or changing the access plan model;
- it is specifically about making the persisted marker file a mandatory part of every successful `access_ready=true` completion path.
acceptance_criteria:
- Successful `access_ready=true` paths always materialize `.gloggur/access_grants.json`, even when `.gloggur` / `.gloggur-cache` already exist and no chmod/mkdir work is needed.
- `gloggur init` repairs the missing marker for already-ready repos both when access is auto-approved and when `--no-grant-access` is used.
- `gloggur access grant` repairs the missing marker for already-ready repos without requiring an access mutation.
- The ready fast path keeps the existing CLI contract shape: `access_ready=true`, `access_result=null`, and no incomplete-access error.
- The persisted state file for the repaired marker remains schema-compatible with the normal grant path (`schema_version = "1"`).
- The shell wrapper path continues to inherit the same success contract through the CLI.
notes:
Current verification evidence gathered 2026-03-26:
- `src/gloggur/access.py:176` defines `persist_ready_access_grant_state(...)`, which exists specifically to persist grant state for a plan that is already ready without mutating access permissions.
- `src/gloggur/access.py:_write_access_grant_result(...)` writes the canonical state payload with `schema_version`, ensuring the ready fast path and normal grant path share one file format.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py:_run_access_grant_flow(...)` calls `persist_ready_access_grant_state(access_plan)` before any prompt or `--yes` handling when `access_plan.access_ready` is already true.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py:_init_impl(...)` separately repairs the marker on the `--no-grant-access` branch when `access_plan.access_ready` is true, which closes the ready-repo hole for `init` as well as `access grant`.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py` locks the exact regression surface:
  - `test_init_json_repairs_missing_access_marker_for_ready_repo`
  - `test_init_json_no_grant_access_repairs_missing_access_marker_for_ready_repo`
  - `test_access_grant_json_yes_repairs_missing_access_marker_for_ready_repo`
  - `test_access_grant_json_without_yes_repairs_missing_access_marker_for_ready_repo`
- Those tests also confirm the public payload contract on the repaired fast path:
  - `access_ready` is `True`
  - `access_result` stays `None`
  - `.gloggur/access_grants.json` exists after the command returns
- `tests/unit/test_access.py::test_apply_access_grant_creates_missing_gloggur_dirs_and_persists_state` confirms the persisted marker payload includes `schema_version == "1"` and `access_ready == true` on the normal mutating path.
- `tests/integration/test_access_wrapper.py::test_access_wrapper_applies_grant_via_cli` confirms the thin shell wrapper preserves the same marker persistence behavior.
- Focused verification run on 2026-03-26:
  - `.venv/bin/pytest -q -n0 tests/unit/test_access.py::test_apply_access_grant_creates_missing_gloggur_dirs_and_persists_state tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py::test_init_json_repairs_missing_access_marker_for_ready_repo tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py::test_init_json_no_grant_access_repairs_missing_access_marker_for_ready_repo tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py::test_access_grant_json_yes_repairs_missing_access_marker_for_ready_repo tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py::test_access_grant_json_without_yes_repairs_missing_access_marker_for_ready_repo tests/integration/test_access_wrapper.py::test_access_wrapper_applies_grant_via_cli`
  - result: `6 passed in 3.23s`
- Gap between the original short issue text and current shipped behavior:
  - the fix is broader than a single `access grant --yes` path;
  - it covers ready-repo repair through both `init` and `access grant`, including the explicit `--no-grant-access` `init` branch;
  - it deliberately preserves the existing success payload shape instead of fabricating a non-null `access_result` for the repair-only path.

title: Fix static quality gate failure on access grant branch
description:
Current repo state shows this closed task was the verification-only cleanup attached to the access-grant feature landing, not a separate access-design change. The access feature itself landed in `b847393` across `src/gloggur/access.py`, `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`, `scripts/grant_gloggur_access.sh`, and the access unit/integration tests. Because `scripts/run_static_quality_gates.py` runs Ruff and Black over the full `src/gloggur` runtime package, the branch also needed to restore static-gate cleanliness before the required verification lane could pass.

The useful interpretation of this issue is therefore: keep the required static-quality harness green while landing the new access planning/grant flow. The current repo already contains the full access contract; this task belongs to the surrounding verification surface that ensured the feature could merge without leaving the required lane red.
design:
- Shared gate surface:
  - `scripts/run_static_quality_gates.py` is the required static-quality runner.
  - `tests/integration/test_run_static_quality_gates_harness.py` and `tests/unit/test_run_static_quality_gates.py` publish the harness contract.
  - `.github/workflows/verification.yml` runs that gate only on the required Python 3.13 lane, and `tests/unit/test_verification_workflow.py::test_verification_workflow_includes_static_quality_gate` locks that workflow step.
- Access feature surface that this cleanup guarded:
  - `src/gloggur/access.py`: `build_access_plan(...)`, `apply_access_grant(...)`, and marker persistence helpers.
  - `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`: `init`, `access plan`, and `access grant` wiring plus structured incomplete-access failure payloads.
  - `scripts/grant_gloggur_access.sh`: thin wrapper over `gloggur access grant`.
  - `tests/unit/test_access.py`, `tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py`, and `tests/integration/test_access_wrapper.py`: regression coverage for the feature itself.
- Scope clarification:
  - this issue is not the access feature contract;
  - it is the static-gate/verification follow-up that kept the access branch mergeable.
acceptance_criteria:
- The access-grant branch passes `scripts/run_static_quality_gates.py --format json` and the integration harness.
- Access feature tests continue to pass after the static-gate cleanup.
- No new access semantics are introduced solely by this cleanup; the public contract remains the one implemented in `src/gloggur/access.py` and `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`.
notes:
Feature landing commit: `b847393` (`feat(init): add explicit repo access grant planning and safe local fixes`).
Current evidence gathered 2026-03-26:
- `git show --stat --summary b847393` shows the access branch touched `src/gloggur/access.py`, `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`, `scripts/grant_gloggur_access.sh`, and the access tests in one landing.
- `tests/unit/test_access.py` still locks planner/apply behavior such as ready plans, blocked paths, and macOS privacy blockers.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py` still locks the main CLI outcomes:
  - `test_init_json_without_yes_returns_access_plan_only_and_fails`
  - `test_init_json_no_grant_access_preserves_success`
  - `test_access_plan_json_returns_deterministic_plan`
  - `test_access_grant_json_yes_applies_and_persists_result`
- `tests/integration/test_access_wrapper.py` still proves the shell wrapper preserves the CLI contract.
- The static-gate harness remains the shared verification lane that this cleanup was restoring.

title: Add explicit repo access grant flow to init
description:
Shipped scope is broader and more concrete than the original one-line description suggests. Current code does not just bolt a `--yes` flag onto `gloggur init`; it introduces an explicit conservative repo-access workflow with three public surfaces: `gloggur init`, `gloggur access plan`, and `gloggur access grant`, plus a thin shell wrapper at `scripts/grant_gloggur_access.sh`.

The feature is designed to make repo-local access setup honest and fail-loud without silently rewriting broad repo permissions. `build_access_plan(...)` inspects the repo plus Glöggur-owned state roots (`.gloggur`, `.gloggur-cache`), classifies blocked paths, distinguishes manual OS privacy blockers from normal permission problems, and proposes only Glöggur-local automatic actions. `init` then incorporates that plan into its success/failure contract, while `access grant` applies the narrow local fixes or returns structured incomplete-access diagnostics.

Observed current state as of 2026-03-26:
- `gloggur init` always writes repo-local config first, then runs or reports the access workflow instead of pretending initialization is fully complete.
- Default JSON `init` without `--yes` fails non-zero with `access_grant_incomplete` when access is still needed, but still returns the computed `access_plan` so callers can inspect the required actions.
- `init --no-grant-access` succeeds while preserving honesty: it returns `initialized=true`, `access_ready=false`, `access_result=null`, and the unresolved access plan instead of mutating access implicitly.
- `access plan` is a pure inspection surface, while `access grant` is the mutating surface.
- Successful grant paths persist state to `.gloggur/access_grants.json`, and the wrapper script simply delegates to the CLI `access grant` flow.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `src/gloggur/access.py`
  - `build_access_plan(...)` is the feature's planner. It scans repo readability/traversability, evaluates `.gloggur` and `.gloggur-cache`, returns deterministic `required_access`, `automatic_actions`, `blocked_paths`, `manual_actions`, and readiness booleans, and marks macOS privacy blockers separately from generic permission failures.
  - `apply_access_grant(...)` is the mutating path. It creates/repairs `.gloggur` and `.gloggur-cache`, rechecks repo blockers, computes manual-action guidance, and persists the grant result under `.gloggur/access_grants.json`.
  - `persist_ready_access_grant_state(...)` and `_write_access_grant_result(...)` make the persisted marker/state file part of the success contract even when no access mutations are required.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`
  - `init` integrates the access workflow into repository initialization.
  - conflicting `--yes` and `--no-grant-access` flags fail with `cli_usage_error`.
  - default JSON/text `init` flows can emit structured/prompted `access_grant_incomplete` outcomes rather than silently succeeding.
  - `_emit_access_incomplete(...)` standardizes the non-zero payload contract with `failure_codes`, `failure_guidance`, and a primary error object.
  - `access plan` exposes the planner directly, and `access grant` reuses the same planner plus `_run_access_grant_flow(...)` to preserve shared semantics.
- `scripts/grant_gloggur_access.sh`
  - is intentionally thin; it delegates directly to `scripts/gloggur access grant ...` so wrapper behavior stays aligned with the CLI contract.
- Docs and error catalog
  - `README.md`, `docs/AGENT_INTEGRATION.md`, `docs/QUICKSTART.md`, and `docs/ERROR_CODES.md` document the operator-facing flow and the `access_grant_incomplete` remediation path.

Important scope clarification:
- this feature is about repo-local Glöggur access setup, not general-purpose repo permission management;
- automatic actions are constrained to Glöggur-owned roots and explicit state repair, while unreadable repo files and OS privacy gates remain explicit manual blockers.
acceptance_criteria:
- `gloggur access plan <path>` returns a deterministic access-plan payload with required access, automatic actions, blocked paths, manual actions, and readiness booleans.
- `gloggur init <path> --json` fails non-zero with `access_grant_incomplete` when access work is still required and the caller did not approve the grant flow.
- `gloggur init <path> --no-grant-access --json` remains a truthful non-mutating success path: repo config is written, `access_ready` reflects reality, and callers receive the unresolved access plan.
- `gloggur access grant <path> --yes --json` applies the Glöggur-local fixes, persists `.gloggur/access_grants.json`, and reports `access_ready=true` when no blockers remain.
- Blocked repo files and macOS privacy blockers are surfaced explicitly in plan/result payloads and feed manual-action guidance instead of being silently ignored.
- The wrapper script `scripts/grant_gloggur_access.sh` preserves the same access-grant contract by delegating directly to the CLI.
notes:
Current verification evidence gathered 2026-03-26:
- `src/gloggur/access.py:build_access_plan(...)` is the core planner and already ships the detailed access contract:
  - repo-root plus Glöggur-owned state requirements under `required_access`
  - automatic actions for `.gloggur` / `.gloggur-cache`
  - `blocked_paths` with structured reasons/details
  - `manual_os_action_required` when darwin privacy blockers are detected
- `tests/unit/test_access.py` locks key planner behaviors:
  - `test_build_access_plan_is_ready_when_repo_and_gloggur_paths_are_writable`
  - `test_build_access_plan_reports_blocked_repo_file`
  - `test_build_access_plan_marks_macos_privacy_blockers`
  - these confirm both the ready path and the blocker classification path.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` shows the user-facing integration contract:
  - `init` rejects conflicting `--yes` and `--no-grant-access`
  - default JSON `init` without approval returns a structured `access_grant_incomplete` failure
  - `init --no-grant-access` succeeds without mutating access and still returns the access plan
  - `access plan` is pure inspection
  - `access grant` is the mutating path
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py` locks those exact CLI semantics:
  - `test_init_json_rejects_conflicting_access_flags`
  - `test_init_json_without_yes_returns_access_plan_only_and_fails`
  - `test_init_json_no_grant_access_preserves_success`
  - `test_access_plan_json_returns_deterministic_plan`
  - `test_access_grant_json_without_yes_returns_incomplete_payload`
  - `test_access_grant_json_yes_applies_and_persists_result`
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py:_emit_access_incomplete(...)` and `docs/ERROR_CODES.md` publish the explicit machine-readable failure code `access_grant_incomplete` plus remediation guidance.
- `scripts/grant_gloggur_access.sh` is intentionally only a delegate to `scripts/gloggur access grant`, and `tests/integration/test_access_wrapper.py::test_access_wrapper_applies_grant_via_cli` confirms it preserves the same success behavior and marker persistence.
- `README.md` and `docs/QUICKSTART.md` already describe the intended operator flow: use `init --yes` to apply Glöggur-local fixes during setup, or `access plan` / the wrapper script when running the access flow separately.
- Focused verification run on 2026-03-26:
  - `.venv/bin/pytest -q -n0 tests/unit/test_access.py::test_build_access_plan_is_ready_when_repo_and_gloggur_paths_are_writable tests/unit/test_access.py::test_build_access_plan_reports_blocked_repo_file tests/unit/test_access.py::test_build_access_plan_marks_macos_privacy_blockers tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py::test_init_json_without_yes_returns_access_plan_only_and_fails tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py::test_init_json_no_grant_access_preserves_success tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py::test_access_plan_json_returns_deterministic_plan tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py::test_access_grant_json_without_yes_returns_incomplete_payload tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py::test_access_grant_json_yes_applies_and_persists_result tests/integration/test_access_wrapper.py::test_access_wrapper_applies_grant_via_cli`
  - result: `9 passed in 3.30s`
- Gap between the original short issue text and current shipped behavior:
  - the feature is broader than "init gained an access grant flow"
  - the shipped contract includes separate plan/apply commands, honest failure guidance, wrapper support, blocker classification, and persisted grant state
  - the design is intentionally conservative: it repairs Glöggur-local state but leaves broader repo/OS permission blockers explicit and manual

title: Refactor staged cursor resume to use canonical staged tables
description:
