- Historical experiment surface from `3d53002`:
  - `src/gloggur/search/router/backends.py`
  - `src/gloggur/search/router/config.py`
  - `src/gloggur/search/router/engine.py`
  - `src/gloggur/search/router/locality.py` (since removed)
  - supporting CLI/docs/tests
- Revert surface from `97636fa`:
  - removes `src/gloggur/search/router/locality.py`
  - strips the locality-specific behavior back out of router, CLI, support, and tests
- Live verification surface that outlasted the experiment:
  - `scripts/run_static_quality_gates.py`
  - `tests/integration/test_run_static_quality_gates_harness.py`
  - `tests/unit/test_run_static_quality_gates.py`
- Scope clarification:
  - current router/search behavior should be described by the surviving router issues and code, not by this historical locality branch cleanup.
acceptance_criteria:
- This issue record clearly distinguishes the short-lived router locality experiment from the router behavior that currently ships.
- The record ties the bug to required-lane static-quality cleanup rather than claiming a still-live locality feature.
- Current code/docs/tests no longer advertise a `locality` router module or locality-specific search contract.
notes:
Historical commits:
- `3d53002` (`fix(router): collapse follow-up searches onto local evidence`)
- `97636fa` (`revert: remove router locality rollout`)
Current evidence gathered 2026-03-26:
- `git show --stat --summary` for those commits shows the locality module was introduced and then fully removed.
- `rg -n "locality" src/gloggur/search/router tests docs` returns no live hits in the current repo.
- The shared static-gate contract remains published by `scripts/run_static_quality_gates.py` and the matching unit/integration harness tests.
- This makes `bd-1bt` a historical verification-cleanup record rather than a description of a current router feature.

title: Collapse router follow-up searches onto local evidence
description:
The issue title describes a feature that briefly landed but is not part of the current shipped router contract. Commit `3d53002` introduced a locality-style follow-up search flow: a short-lived local anchor, stronger first-hit locality ranking, nearby-test bridging, and additive ContextPack locality/debug data. That entire locality rollout was later removed by `97636fa`, so the current repo has no `src/gloggur/search/router/locality.py`, no persisted local anchor, and no locality fields in router outputs.

This tracker record is most useful as historical provenance for the reverted experiment. Current search improvements in the repo now come from other contracts such as intent/hint separation, exact-backend fallback hardening, grep-compatible parsing, and bounded `find --about` behavior, not from the superseded locality mode described in the original stub.
design:
- Original experiment surface from `git show --stat --summary 3d53002`:
  - `src/gloggur/search/router/locality.py`
  - router updates in `src/gloggur/search/router/engine.py`, `src/gloggur/search/router/backends.py`, and `src/gloggur/search/router/config.py`
  - CLI/support/docs/test updates intended to expose locality/debug behavior
- Revert surface from `git show --stat --summary 97636fa`:
  - removes `src/gloggur/search/router/locality.py`
  - restores the router/support/test surfaces to the pre-locality contract
- Current live boundary:
  - `src/gloggur/search/router/engine.py`, `src/gloggur/search/router/backends.py`, and `src/gloggur/search/router/types.py` contain no locality anchor or locality-follow-up execution path.
acceptance_criteria:
This issue record explicitly distinguishes the reverted locality experiment from the router behavior that currently ships.
No current code or docs imply that repo-local anchors, locality ranking fields, or locality-specific ContextPack metadata are still available.
Historical provenance for the experiment is preserved through the commit references and dependency link to the rollback issue.
notes:
The feature described here briefly landed in `3d53002` and was then removed by `97636fa`; the current repo no longer ships that locality workflow.
Related rollback record: `bd-5ae`.
Verification on 2026-03-26 used `git show --stat --summary` for both commits plus a live grep over `src/gloggur`, `tests`, and `docs` confirming the locality surfaces are gone.

title: Harden public docs/help against promise gaps
description:
This issue now maps to a public-surface contract hardening pass: docs and `--help` must not promise features that the current CLI intentionally removed or narrowed. In current code, the old search grounding flags (`--with-evidence-trace`, `--validate-grounding`, `--evidence-min-confidence`, `--evidence-min-items`, `--fail-on-ungrounded`) still exist only as hidden compatibility flags in `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` and fail closed with `error.code=search_contract_v1_removed`. The public docs instead describe ContextPack v2, bidirectional graph neighbors by default, structural graph edges by default, baseline-not-uniform parser support, and the fact that quickstart/CI mostly use `GLOGGUR_EMBEDDING_PROVIDER=test` rather than validating local-model bootstrap.

The real problem behind the original title was not missing docs volume; it was drift between public promises and shipped behavior. The current value of this issue is an honest operator contract across README, agent docs, quickstart, verification docs, and the CLI help surface.
design:
Current implementation touchpoints:
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`: the legacy grounding flags remain present but `hidden=True`, and misuse routes into the `search_contract_v1_removed` compatibility/error path instead of being advertised as supported current workflow.
- `docs/AGENT_INTEGRATION.md`: explicitly tells operators that the removed grounding flags now fail closed with `error.code=search_contract_v1_removed`.
- `README.md`: documents graph-neighbor directionality, structural-vs-embedded graph-edge behavior, parser known-gap honesty, and the deterministic test-provider scope.
- `docs/QUICKSTART.md` and `docs/VERIFICATION.md`: explain current gotchas around `--embed-graph-edges`, repo-config warnings, and the fact that most deterministic CI paths use `GLOGGUR_EMBEDDING_PROVIDER=test` rather than validating local bootstrap.
- `tests/unit/test_public_docs_contract.py`: is the current regression net for these public-surface promises.

The important boundary is that this is a contract-hygiene issue, not a feature-delivery issue. The fix is to make docs/help truthful about the current CLI surface and removed compatibility paths.
acceptance_criteria:
- Public docs do not advertise removed v1 grounding flags as normal supported workflow.
- The agent guide explains that the removed grounding flags now fail closed with `search_contract_v1_removed`.
- README/quickstart docs describe current graph defaults, parser-gap honesty, and deterministic test-provider scope accurately.
- The hidden compatibility flags remain hidden in CLI help while preserving explicit fail-closed compatibility behavior if used.
- Public-docs regression tests cover the main promise-gap surfaces.
notes:
Current verification evidence gathered 2026-03-26:
- `tests/unit/test_public_docs_contract.py` currently locks the public-surface contract through:
  - `test_readme_and_agent_guide_do_not_advertise_removed_grounding_flags`
  - `test_readme_documents_bidirectional_graph_neighbors_and_parser_gaps`
  - `test_quickstart_documents_current_operator_gotchas`
  - `test_verification_docs_and_harness_expose_test_provider_scope`
- Direct repo grep confirms the live docs/help state:
  - `docs/AGENT_INTEGRATION.md` documents `search_contract_v1_removed`
  - `README.md` documents bidirectional graph neighbors, structural graph-edge defaults, parser baseline limits, and `GLOGGUR_EMBEDDING_PROVIDER=test` scope
  - `docs/QUICKSTART.md` and `docs/VERIFICATION.md` both describe the deterministic test-provider boundary
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` still defines the old grounding flags at the search/find entrypoints, but they are marked `hidden=True` rather than being part of the public help contract.

title: Relax default tool-version drift compatibility for reusable indexes
description:
Default compatibility no longer treats pure tool-version drift as a hard blocker. In `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`, `_tool_version_reindex_reason(...)` detects drift, but `_build_search_health_snapshot(...)` feeds it into `warning_codes` instead of `needs_reindex` when schema, profile, reset, and integrity state are otherwise reusable. `status --json` and `search --json` therefore stay `resume_ok` while still surfacing `last_success_tool_version_match`, drift flags, and provenance fields.

The current contract is narrower than the original title suggests: only tool-version skew was relaxed. Schema rebuilds, missing metadata, interrupted builds, profile drift, and integrity failures still block reuse. This bead should be read as the shipped advisory-drift compatibility policy plus its regression coverage, not as blanket tolerance for arbitrary cache skew.
design:
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`: `_tool_version_reindex_reason(...)`, `_build_resume_contract(...)`, and `_build_search_health_snapshot(...)` define the advisory drift semantics and payload fields (`tool_version_drift_detected`, `allow_tool_version_drift`, `tool_version_drift_override_applied`, and `last_success_tool_version_match`).
- `docs/ERROR_CODES.md` and `docs/BONKERS_SEARCH_FAILURE_MATRIX.md` publish `tool_version_changed` and explain that it is advisory by default.
- `tests/integration/test_resume_contract_integration.py` is the main end-to-end regression surface for status/search behavior across adjacent version drift.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py` covers the lower-level reason and payload wiring.
- The explicit `--allow-tool-version-drift` flag and `GLOGGUR_ALLOW_TOOL_VERSION_DRIFT` env var remain accepted for compatibility, but under the current contract they are no-op inputs when the only issue is pure tool-version drift.
acceptance_criteria:
- Pure tool-version drift leaves `resume_decision = resume_ok` and `needs_reindex = false` in both `status --json` and `search --json` when other compatibility checks still pass.
- Drift is still surfaced explicitly through `warning_codes`, match fields, and provenance metadata instead of being silently ignored.
- A successful no-op reindex repairs the stored last-success tool-version marker without perturbing stable resume fingerprints.
- Schema, profile, reset, interruption, and integrity incompatibilities remain hard blockers; this issue does not weaken those checks.
- Docs and regression tests describe `--allow-tool-version-drift` as compatibility input retention, not as the mechanism that now enables default reuse.
notes:
- `tests/integration/test_resume_contract_integration.py::test_status_and_search_keep_resume_ok_when_only_last_success_tool_version_drifts` locks the default advisory behavior.
- `tests/integration/test_resume_contract_integration.py::test_resume_recovers_after_successful_noop_reindex_when_only_tool_version_marker_drifts` locks marker repair after a successful no-op reindex.
- `tests/integration/test_resume_contract_integration.py::test_resume_accepts_explicit_tool_version_drift_override_input_as_noop` and `::test_resume_accepts_tool_version_drift_override_from_env_var_as_noop` show the flag/env inputs are retained without changing the default outcome.
- `docs/ERROR_CODES.md` now documents `tool_version_changed` and `tool_version_changed_override` separately, with the default contract centered on warnings plus standard resume metadata.
- This issue is best interpreted as a compatibility-policy narrowing: version drift alone is advisory; other incompatibilities still force rebuilds.
close_reason: Closed after fixing static quality-gates follow-up formatting issues

title: Allow tool_version_changed to self-heal after successful reindex
description: Current code shows this landed as a resume-marker repair, not a blanket cache-reset workaround. `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` still treats pure tool-version drift as an advisory warning (`tool_version_changed`) while keeping `resume_decision = "resume_ok"` when the committed cache is otherwise reusable. The actual repair path lives in `_persist_last_success_resume_state(...)`: after a successful index run, it rewrites only the stale tool-version marker when the stored resume fingerprint already matches, rewrites the legacy fingerprint in place when needed, and updates the full last-success marker set only when the stale fingerprint actually requires it. The result is that a successful no-op reindex can self-heal marker-only tool-version drift without deleting caches or fabricating a full rebuild.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `src/gloggur/indexer/cache.py`
  - persists `last_success_tool_version` as a first-class cache marker.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`
  - `_tool_version_reindex_reason(...)` classifies tool-version drift as a distinct diagnostic.
  - `_build_resume_contract(...)` keeps pure tool-version drift advisory by emitting warning metadata while preserving `resume_ok` when the rest of the cache is reusable.
  - `_persist_last_success_resume_state(...)` is the core repair path for marker-only drift, legacy fingerprint normalization, and unchanged-run idempotence.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py` and `tests/integration/test_resume_contract_integration.py`
  - pin advisory warning behavior, no-op repair after successful reindex, and the no-change timestamp/idempotence boundary.
- `docs/ERROR_CODES.md` and `docs/BONKERS_SEARCH_FAILURE_MATRIX.md`
  - document `tool_version_changed` as a warning-style resume surface rather than a hard stop.

Scope clarification:
- this issue does not make real incompatibilities soft;
- it only self-heals the marker-only tool-version drift case after a successful index run.
acceptance_criteria:
- Marker-only tool-version drift leaves `resume_decision = "resume_ok"` and emits `tool_version_changed` as warning metadata instead of forcing reindex-required state.
- A successful no-op reindex repairs stale tool-version markers without requiring cache deletion.
- When the stored fingerprint already matches, only the tool-version marker is rewritten; unchanged-run timestamps remain stable.
- Legacy fingerprint formats are normalized in place without unnecessary timestamp churn.
- Regression coverage pins advisory status/search behavior and the repair path for successful reindex runs.
notes:
Current evidence on 2026-03-26:
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` still exposes `tool_version_changed` and `tool_version_changed_override` guidance and still repairs marker-only drift through `_persist_last_success_resume_state(...)`.
- `tests/integration/test_resume_contract_integration.py` still proves pure tool-version drift remains `resume_ok` plus warning metadata and is repaired by a successful no-op reindex.
Focused verification on 2026-03-26:
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_persist_last_success_resume_state_no_change_reindex_is_idempotent`
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_persist_last_success_resume_state_repairs_only_tool_version_marker`
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_persist_last_success_resume_state_repairs_tool_version_drift_with_stale_fingerprint`
- `tests/integration/test_resume_contract_integration.py::test_resume_warns_when_last_success_tool_version_is_tampered`
- `tests/integration/test_resume_contract_integration.py::test_resume_recovers_after_successful_noop_reindex_when_only_tool_version_marker_drifts`
- included in the focused pytest batch -> `30 passed in 8.84s`

title: Fix watch daemon readiness race in CI
description:
Original bug: daemon-mode `watch start --daemon --json` could report a live/running watch too early, before the child process had actually armed the watcher backend and become capable of consuming save events. In CI this surfaced as a race in the watch lifecycle integration test: startup looked successful, but the first file update or follow-on `search` could run against a watch process that had only spawned, not reached a stable ready state.

The shipped fix in commit `f5ea404` changed this from an implicit timing assumption into an explicit readiness contract. `src/gloggur/watch/service.py` now writes `ready=false`, `ready_at=null`, and `status="starting"` before entering the watcher loop, and only flips to `ready=true` with a populated `ready_at` once the first watcher event arrives and the backend is actually armed. `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` then makes daemon startup wait for that ready payload via `_wait_for_watch_daemon_ready(...)` and `_watch_ready_state_payload(...)` before returning a successful `watch start --daemon --json` response.

Current behavior is more concrete than the original issue text: daemon startup no longer treats “child process exists” as sufficient, startup timeouts normalize to a structured `verify watch daemon startup` failure and persist `status="failed_startup"` with `ready=false`, and `watch status --json` / downstream readiness probes consume the same `ready` + `ready_at` contract. The lifecycle integration test was also hardened to wait on that ready state and to use the same `--config` path for follow-on `search`, so CI no longer races startup against a mismatched config/runtime path.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `src/gloggur/watch/service.py`:
  - `run_forever(...)` writes an initial state payload with `running=true`, `ready=false`, `ready_at=None`, and `status="starting"` before the watcher backend is armed.
  - once the watch loop receives its first event and the backend is actually active, it writes `ready=true`, stamps `ready_at`, and transitions status to `running` or `running_with_errors`.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`:
  - daemon mode spawns the child, writes a starting state payload, then blocks in `_wait_for_watch_daemon_ready(...)` instead of immediately returning success.
  - `_watch_ready_state_payload(...)` defines the minimum ready contract: matching pid, `running=true`, `ready=true`, non-empty `ready_at`, and status in `{running, running_with_errors}`.
  - `_fail_watch_daemon_startup(...)` persists `status="failed_startup"` / `ready=false` and raises a structured `StorageIOError` with operation `verify watch daemon startup` when the daemon exits early or never reports readiness.
  - `_watch_status_ready(...)` and `watch status --json` consume the same explicit ready signal instead of inferring readiness from liveness alone.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_watch.py`: locks both the success path (`watch start` returns `ready=true` with `ready_at`) and the timeout path (`failed_startup` plus structured startup verification error).
- `tests/integration/test_watch_cli_lifecycle_integration.py`: uses the daemon’s explicit ready contract before mutating the watched file and runs `search` with the same `--config` path to keep runtime/cache selection aligned.
- `scripts/check_startup_readiness.py`: the repo’s startup probe now validates the live `watch status --json` payload against this normalized readiness/state contract.

Scope boundary:
- this issue is about startup/readiness truthfulness and test determinism, not about later incremental failure synthesis or watch batch error contracts.
acceptance_criteria:
- `watch start --daemon --json` returns success only after the daemon child has reported an explicit ready state, not merely after the process is spawned.
- The watch state file records `ready=false`, `ready_at=null`, and `status="starting"` before the backend is armed, then transitions to `ready=true` with a non-empty `ready_at` once startup is actually complete.
- Daemon startup timeout or early exit fails with a structured `verify watch daemon startup` error and persists `status="failed_startup"` / `ready=false` in the state file.
- `watch status --json` and helper readiness checks use the explicit `ready` / `ready_at` contract instead of inferring readiness from pid liveness alone.
- The watch lifecycle integration flow waits for the same ready contract before expecting the first file update to appear in search results.
- Follow-on search in the lifecycle integration path uses the same `--config` path as the watch commands so runtime state and search target the same repo/cache configuration.
notes:
Current verification evidence gathered 2026-03-26:
- Landing provenance:
  - commit `f5ea404` (`fix(watch): wait for daemon readiness before reporting running`) introduced the daemon-start readiness wait, explicit ready-state payload contract, `failed_startup` normalization, and expanded unit/integration coverage.
  - commit `299345a` (`test(watch): make watch lifecycle integration deterministic on CI`) aligned the lifecycle integration test with the startup/readiness contract and CI expectations.
- Current code inspection:
  - `src/gloggur/watch/service.py:run_forever(...)` writes `ready=false` / `status="starting"` first, then flips to `ready=true` with a populated `ready_at` only after the watch loop is armed.
  - `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` daemon startup now calls `_wait_for_watch_daemon_ready(...)` and only emits `started=true` after `_watch_ready_state_payload(...)` accepts the state payload.
  - `src/gloggur/cli/main.py:_fail_watch_daemon_startup(...)` writes `status="failed_startup"`, `ready=false`, and `ready_at=None` before raising a structured startup verification error.
  - `tests/integration/test_watch_cli_lifecycle_integration.py::test_watch_lifecycle_commands_with_env_overrides` asserts `started_payload["ready"] is True`, `started_payload["ready_at"]` is populated, and the follow-on `search` uses the same `--config` path while waiting for the updated file to appear.
  - `scripts/check_startup_readiness.py` validates the live watch payload as part of the repo’s startup readiness probe.
- Focused verification run:
  - `./.venv/bin/pytest -q -n0 tests/unit/test_cli_watch.py::test_watch_start_json_reports_structured_daemon_readiness_timeout tests/unit/test_cli_watch.py::test_watch_start_daemon_and_stop tests/integration/test_watch_cli_lifecycle_integration.py::test_watch_lifecycle_commands_with_env_overrides`
  - result: `3 passed, 3 warnings in 6.36s`
- Direct probes:
  - `_watch_ready_state_payload({'pid': 42, 'running': True, 'ready': False, 'ready_at': None, 'status': 'starting'}, expected_pid=42) -> None`
  - `_watch_ready_state_payload({'pid': 42, 'running': True, 'ready': True, 'ready_at': '2026-03-09T11:20:00+00:00', 'status': 'running'}, expected_pid=42)` returns the normalized ready payload.
  - `_watch_status_ready({'ready': False, 'status': 'running'}, running=True) -> False`
  - `_watch_status_ready({'ready': True, 'status': 'running'}, running=True) -> True`
- Behavioral boundary clarified by the current code:
  - the shipped fix does not make watch startup instant; it makes startup honest. Success is delayed until the backend is really ready, and failures surface as `failed_startup` instead of a misleading live/running payload.

title: Include byte offsets in find output
description:
Current code already ships additive byte offsets on the agent-first `find` surface. `gloggur find --json` and `gloggur find --stream` now expose `start_byte` and `end_byte` on each emitted hit when the routed search result includes byte spans, allowing agents to round-trip directly into `gloggur extract` without requesting the bulkier `search --json` contract or extra flags.

This change is narrower than “search payload redesign”. `find` still emits the slim `find_v1` contract with `schema_version = 1`, a `decision` block, and a truncated hit list optimized for locator workflows. The byte offsets are an additive field on the per-hit payload, not a contract-family migration. Plain-text `find` output remains the compact rg-like summary and does not print byte offsets.

The current implementation is also broader than a pure CLI formatting tweak. Byte offsets originate upstream in routed hits from the exact, semantic, and symbol backends, are preserved by `ContextHit`, and are then forwarded by the `find` serializers. The practical user-facing effect is that `find` can now serve as the lightweight discovery step in a `find -> extract` workflow without forcing agents to fall back to `search` just to recover byte ranges.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `src/gloggur/search/router/backends.py`
  - exact, semantic, and symbol backends already attach `start_byte` / `end_byte` to backend hits when byte-span information is available.
- `src/gloggur/search/router/engine.py`
  - `_pack_hits(...)` preserves those byte offsets when projecting backend hits into `ContextHit` objects.
- `src/gloggur/search/router/types.py`
  - `ContextHit` models `start_byte` and `end_byte` as additive optional fields and includes them in `to_dict()` when present.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`
  - `_build_find_hit_payload(...)` is the narrow feature gate for this issue: it copies `start_byte` / `end_byte` into the slim `find` hit payload when they are integers.
  - `_build_find_success_payload(...)` uses that helper for `find --json`.
  - `_emit_find_success(...)` reuses the same hit payload objects for NDJSON stream mode and keeps plain-text output rg-like by rendering only rank/path/line/score/tags/snippet.
- `src/gloggur/search/compat.py`
  - the legacy search compatibility adapter already maps byte offsets from `hits[]` into compatibility `results[]`, so this issue should be understood as `find` parity with the routed byte-span model rather than a new byte-span primitive.
- docs
  - `README.md` and `docs/AGENT_INTEGRATION.md` now describe byte-offset availability and the `search/find -> extract` handoff.

Recommended scope interpretation:
- treat this issue as a shipped `find` contract refinement on top of `bd-061`, not as a new extraction subsystem;
- keep future payload growth constrained so `find` stays materially slimmer than `search --json` even while carrying the minimum fields needed for agent round-trips.
acceptance_criteria:
- `gloggur find --json` includes additive `start_byte` and `end_byte` fields on hits by default when routed hits carry byte spans.
- `gloggur find --stream` emits the same per-hit byte offsets in NDJSON mode.
- Plain-text `gloggur find` output remains unchanged in shape and does not expose byte offsets.
- `gloggur search --json` remains on the ContextPack v2 contract and still carries byte offsets; this follow-up does not collapse `search` and `find` into one payload family.
- A hit path plus `start_byte` / `end_byte` can be fed directly into `gloggur extract ... --json` to recover the exact UTF-8 slice represented by the hit.
- Docs and CLI tests describe the inclusive/exclusive byte contract (`start_byte` inclusive, `end_byte` exclusive) and the agent round-trip workflow.
notes:
Current verification evidence gathered 2026-03-26:
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py:_build_find_hit_payload(...)` conditionally includes `start_byte` and `end_byte` on the slim `find` hit payload.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py:_emit_find_success(...)` streams those same hit payload objects in NDJSON mode and keeps plain-text output to rank/path/line/score/tags/snippet without printing byte offsets.
- `src/gloggur/search/router/engine.py:_pack_hits(...)` copies `start_byte=hit.start_byte` and `end_byte=hit.end_byte` into `ContextHit`, confirming the CLI is forwarding routed byte spans rather than inventing them.
- `src/gloggur/search/router/types.py:ContextHit` already models byte offsets as first-class optional hit fields.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_find_json_contract_is_slim_and_security_gated` confirms `find --json` stays on `schema_version == 1` / `contract_version == "find_v1"` while emitting hit-level `start_byte == 10` and `end_byte == 18`.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_find_stream_emits_ndjson_hits` confirms `find --stream` emits NDJSON hits with `start_byte` and `end_byte` and no top-level `decision` object per line.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_search_json_contract_remains_contextpack_v2_after_find_addition` confirms `search --json` still uses the separate ContextPack v2 surface while retaining byte offsets.
- `tests/integration/test_cli.py::test_cli_search_json_emits_contextpack_v2_without_legacy_keys` confirms indexed real CLI output includes `start_byte` / `end_byte` on hits and excludes removed v1 keys.
- `tests/integration/test_cli.py::test_cli_search_extract_round_trip_for_exact_semantic_and_symbol_modes` proves the practical round-trip: hit `path`, `start_byte`, and `end_byte` feed `gloggur extract ... --json`, and the extracted text matches the raw byte slice.
- Docs touchpoints already landed:
  - `README.md` documents `start_byte` / `end_byte` on search hits and explicitly notes that `find --json` includes byte offsets for exact agent round-trips.
  - `docs/AGENT_INTEGRATION.md` documents the `search/find -> extract` workflow and the inclusive/exclusive byte semantics.
- Focused verification run on 2026-03-26:
  - `.venv/bin/pytest -q -n0 tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_find_json_contract_is_slim_and_security_gated tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_find_stream_emits_ndjson_hits tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_search_json_contract_remains_contextpack_v2_after_find_addition tests/integration/test_cli.py::test_cli_search_json_emits_contextpack_v2_without_legacy_keys tests/integration/test_cli.py::test_cli_search_extract_round_trip_for_exact_semantic_and_symbol_modes`
  - result: `5 passed, 3 warnings in 3.18s`
- Gap between the original short issue text and current shipped behavior:
  - the original wording sounded like a serializer-only field addition;
  - the actual shipped contract spans routed byte-span propagation, slim find hit serialization, NDJSON stream parity, extract interoperability, and explicit preservation of separate `find_v1` vs `search` ContextPack v2 surfaces.

title: Add agent-first find command
description: Current code shows `find` is now a first-class agent-oriented command rather than a thin alias for `search`. `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` runs both commands through `_execute_search_command(...)`, but `find` hard-codes `result_profile="locator"`, `entrypoint="find_cli_v1"`, and `contract_version="find_v1"`, then projects routed hits through `_build_find_success_payload(...)` and `_emit_find_success(...)` into a much slimmer text/JSON/NDJSON surface. The shipped contract includes decision statuses (`decisive`, `ambiguous`, `no_match`, `suppressed`), truncated snippets, default hit capping, optional suggested-next-command hints, and additive byte offsets on hits, while `search` remains on the fuller `contextpack_v2` payload. This issue is best read as the landing of a separate locator-first agent surface on top of shared router execution, not as a rename or formatting tweak to `search`.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`
  - `_execute_search_command(...)` is the shared execution helper used by both `search` and `find`.
  - `find` passes `result_profile="locator"`, `entrypoint="find_cli_v1"`, and `contract_version="find_v1"`.
  - `_build_find_hit_payload(...)`, `_build_find_success_payload(...)`, and `_emit_find_success(...)` define the slim `find` contract and its text/JSON/stream serializers.
- `docs/state-diagrams/find-command-state.md`
  - documents the dedicated `find_v1` decision surface and how it differs from `contextpack_v2`.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py`
  - pins terse text statuses, slim JSON output, NDJSON stream mode, shared router invocation semantics, and the size split between `find` and `search`.

Scope clarification:
- this feature did not replace `search`;
- it created a separate agent-facing locator contract that intentionally stays slimmer than `search --json`.
acceptance_criteria:
- `gloggur find` remains a distinct command surface with `contract_version = "find_v1"` while `gloggur search` stays on `contextpack_v2`.
- Text, `--json`, and `--stream` outputs all work off the same routed result set.
- The `find` payload exposes only locator-oriented fields (`decision`, slim `hits`, additive warnings/security warnings) instead of the full search metadata surface.
- Byte offsets stay additive on `find` hits without collapsing `find` and `search` into one contract family.
- Regression coverage proves `find` remains materially smaller than `search --json` for the same routed results.
notes:
Current evidence on 2026-03-26:
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` still routes `find` through `_execute_search_command(...)` but serializes it as `find_v1` via `_build_find_success_payload(...)` and `_emit_find_success(...)`.
- `docs/state-diagrams/find-command-state.md` still documents the dedicated `find_v1` state surface.
Focused verification on 2026-03-26:
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_find_text_output_reports_decision_statuses`
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_find_json_contract_is_slim_and_security_gated`
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_find_stream_emits_ndjson_hits`
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_find_matches_search_router_invocation_semantics`
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_find_json_is_smaller_than_search_json_for_same_results`
- included in the focused pytest batch -> `30 passed in 8.84s`

title: Artifact provenance and restore trust controls
description:
The shipped artifact hardening is narrower and more specific than the original title suggests. Current code extends artifact manifests with lightweight provenance metadata, reuses a validate-first restore path, preserves file-hash and totals verification, and keeps restore-path traversal protections fail-closed. The strict trust controls are opt-in at restore time rather than universally enforced for every artifact.

The main implementation lives in `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`. `artifact publish` builds a deterministic `manifest.json` with cache compatibility metadata plus a `provenance` block from `_build_artifact_provenance()`. That block records the Glöggur tool version, creation context (`local` vs `ci`), the current git commit when available, and GitHub Actions run metadata when present.

`artifact validate` and `artifact restore` then share the same verification path through `_validate_artifact_archive(...)`. That validator checks archive existence, parses `manifest.json`, enforces `manifest_schema_version`, verifies per-file hashes and byte counts, checks aggregate totals, optionally enforces an out-of-band manifest digest via `--expected-manifest-sha256`, and computes provenance warning/error outcomes through `_artifact_provenance_warning_codes(...)`.

The important boundary is that missing provenance is not a blanket hard failure. Without `--require-provenance`, validate/restore return `warning_codes=['artifact_manifest_provenance_missing']` for legacy artifacts. With `--require-provenance`, the same case fails closed with `artifact_manifest_provenance_missing`. Restore also preserves the older structural hardening: `_resolve_artifact_restore_path(...)` rejects absolute or escaping member paths, and `_restore_artifact_archive(...)` restores into a staging directory before activation rather than unpacking directly into the destination.
design:
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`
  - `_build_artifact_manifest(...)` packages deterministic cache metadata and always includes `provenance: _build_artifact_provenance()` for newly published artifacts.
  - `_build_artifact_provenance()` records `tool_version`, `creation_context`, optional `git_commit`, and optional GitHub Actions fields (`repository`, `run_id`, `run_attempt`, `ref`, `sha`).
  - `_validate_artifact_archive(...)` is the shared trust/integrity gate for both `artifact validate` and `artifact restore`.
    - verifies archive readability
    - requires `manifest.json`
    - enforces manifest schema version
    - verifies file-level hashes/bytes and manifest totals
    - optionally enforces `expected_manifest_sha256`
    - delegates provenance handling to `_artifact_provenance_warning_codes(...)`
  - `_artifact_provenance_warning_codes(...)` is where the soft-vs-strict policy lives:
    - missing provenance -> warning in normal mode
    - missing provenance -> `artifact_manifest_provenance_missing` error when `require_provenance=True`
  - `_resolve_artifact_restore_path(...)` preserves traversal defenses by rejecting absolute or escaping restore paths.
  - `_restore_artifact_archive(...)` restores through a staging directory and only activates the destination after successful extraction.
- Tests/docs covering the shipped contract:
  - `tests/integration/test_cli.py`
  - `docs/state-diagrams/artifact-restore-state.md`
  - `docs/state-diagrams/artifact-validate-state.md`
  - `docs/ERROR_CODES.md`

Important scope clarification:
- this issue shipped provenance capture plus restore-time enforcement hooks;
- it did not make provenance mandatory for every legacy artifact by default;
- hash, totals, and traversal protections remained part of the same validation path rather than being replaced by provenance checks.
acceptance_criteria:
Published artifacts embed a `manifest.json` containing deterministic cache metadata plus a provenance block describing tool version and creation context.
`artifact validate` and `artifact restore` share the same manifest/file verification path, including manifest schema enforcement, file hash/byte verification, and manifest totals checks.
`--expected-manifest-sha256` fails closed when the archive manifest digest does not match the supplied value.
Missing provenance remains a soft warning on validate/restore by default, but `--require-provenance` turns that condition into a hard `artifact_manifest_provenance_missing` failure.
Restore keeps traversal and activation hardening intact by rejecting escaping paths and restoring via a staging directory before moving into place.
notes:
Landed in commit `5c90c04` (`feat: harden mixed-trust config, artifact, and release flows`).
Focused verification on 2026-03-26:
- `./.venv/bin/pytest -q -n0 tests/integration/test_cli.py::test_cli_artifact_publish_packages_cache_with_manifest tests/integration/test_cli.py::test_cli_artifact_restore_restores_cache_for_downstream_search tests/integration/test_cli.py::test_cli_artifact_restore_rejects_missing_provenance_in_enforce_mode tests/integration/test_cli.py::test_cli_artifact_restore_rejects_manifest_sha256_mismatch` -> `4 passed, 3 warnings in 4.15s`
Concrete shipped behavior from those nodes and current code:
- publish emits `manifest_schema_version`, `manifest_sha256`, and a provenance block
- restore succeeds with `--require-provenance --expected-manifest-sha256 <published_digest>` on current artifacts
- removing `provenance` from the manifest makes strict restore fail with `artifact_manifest_provenance_missing`
- supplying the wrong `--expected-manifest-sha256` makes restore fail with `artifact_manifest_sha256_mismatch`
- docs in `docs/state-diagrams/artifact-restore-state.md` explicitly note that restore reuses validate logic, including provenance checks and expected manifest digest enforcement
Gap between the original low-signal wording and current shipped behavior:
- provenance is implemented and enforced on the strict path today
- the default path still allows legacy artifacts with warning codes instead of universal hard failure
- restore-time trust controls are layered on top of existing manifest/hash/traversal verification, not a separate replacement mechanism

title: Remote provider and custom endpoint trust controls
description:
The shipped contract is narrower and more concrete than the original title. This work did not ban remote embeddings outright; it added mixed-trust provenance warnings, surfaced remote-provider/host diagnostics in CLI JSON payloads, and made non-default OpenAI-compatible endpoints fail closed unless the operator explicitly opts in.

Current behavior is split across config resolution and provider initialization:
- `src/gloggur/config.py` classifies repo-local `.gloggur.yaml` / `.env` configuration as untrusted by default when `GLOGGUR_REPO_CONFIG_TRUST=auto`, and computes stable `security_warning_codes` such as `untrusted_repo_config`, `untrusted_remote_provider_requested`, and `custom_embedding_endpoint_requested`.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` decorates JSON payloads with `config_source`, `config_trust_mode`, `security_warning_codes`, and `remote_embedding` diagnostics so operators can see both the provenance of the active config and the remote host that would receive embeddings.
- `src/gloggur/embeddings/openai.py` enforces the hard fail-closed part: custom OpenAI-compatible base URLs are rejected unless `GLOGGUR_ALLOW_CUSTOM_EMBEDDING_ENDPOINTS=1` is set. Built-in defaults (`https://api.openai.com/v1` and the OpenRouter default) remain allowed without that override.
- `src/gloggur/embeddings/gemini.py` and `src/gloggur/config.py` publish the remote destination host (`gemini.googleapis.com` or the resolved OpenAI/OpenRouter host) for diagnostics, but the trust signal itself remains warning metadata rather than a blanket runtime deny.

That means the shipped policy is: explicit operator intent remains available, repo-local/untrusted remote-provider requests are called out loudly in machine-readable metadata, and custom endpoint routing requires an explicit env opt-in instead of silently trusting arbitrary proxy endpoints.
design:
- `src/gloggur/config.py`
  - `DEFAULT_REPO_CONFIG_TRUST_MODE`, `REPO_CONFIG_TRUST_MODES`, and `_compute_security_warning_codes(...)` define the trust/warning contract.
  - auto-discovered repo config and repo-local `.env` inputs are classified as untrusted in `auto` mode; explicit `--config` paths remain trusted unless the operator forces `GLOGGUR_REPO_CONFIG_TRUST=untrusted`.
  - `remote_embedding_destination()` and `requests_custom_embedding_endpoint()` provide the stable diagnostics consumed by the CLI layer.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`
  - `_resolve_repo_config_trust_mode()` fails closed on invalid env values with `repo_config_trust_env_invalid`.
  - `_decorate_payload_with_security_metadata(...)` attaches `config_source`, `config_trust_mode`, `security_warning_codes`, and `remote_embedding` to JSON payloads and nested summary/debug sections.
  - `_remote_embedding_diagnostics(...)` prefers live provider endpoint info when a provider instance exists, otherwise falls back to config-derived host inference.
- `src/gloggur/embeddings/openai.py`
  - `_enforce_allowed_base_url(...)` is the runtime enforcement point for custom OpenAI-compatible endpoints.
  - default OpenAI and OpenRouter endpoints are allowed; non-default endpoints require `GLOGGUR_ALLOW_CUSTOM_EMBEDDING_ENDPOINTS=1`.
- `src/gloggur/embeddings/gemini.py`
  - exposes `endpoint_host = "gemini.googleapis.com"` so remote-destination diagnostics stay stable for Gemini.
- Tests/docs for the shipped contract live in `tests/unit/test_config.py`, `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py`, `tests/unit/test_embeddings.py`, and `docs/QUICKSTART.md` / `docs/ERROR_CODES.md`.

Important scope clarification:
- this issue shipped warning/reporting controls plus endpoint opt-in enforcement;
- it did not make auto-discovered remote providers universally fail to initialize just because the repo config is untrusted.
acceptance_criteria:
Auto-discovered repo config that selects a remote embedding provider emits machine-readable trust warnings (`untrusted_repo_config`, `untrusted_remote_provider_requested`) instead of silently looking trusted.
JSON CLI payloads surface config provenance and remote-destination diagnostics through `config_source`, `config_trust_mode`, `security_warning_codes`, and `remote_embedding`.
Explicit config paths continue to work as trusted operator intent in the default `auto` trust mode and therefore do not emit the untrusted repo warning by default.
Custom non-default OpenAI-compatible endpoints fail closed unless `GLOGGUR_ALLOW_CUSTOM_EMBEDDING_ENDPOINTS=1` is set.
Invalid `GLOGGUR_REPO_CONFIG_TRUST` env values fail closed with a stable `repo_config_trust_env_invalid` contract.
notes:
Landed in commit `5c90c04` (`feat: harden mixed-trust config, artifact, and release flows`).
The shipped boundary is important: untrusted repo-local remote-provider requests are warned and surfaced, but not blanket-blocked; the hard deny is specifically for non-default OpenAI-compatible endpoints without operator opt-in.
Direct probe on 2026-03-26:
- auto-discovered `.gloggur.yaml` with `embedding_provider: openai` and `openai_base_url: https://proxy.example.test/v1` returned exit `0` from `status --json` with `config_source=auto_discovered`, `config_trust_mode=auto`, `security_warning_codes=['custom_embedding_endpoint_requested', 'untrusted_remote_provider_requested', 'untrusted_repo_config']`, and `remote_embedding={'provider': 'openai', 'host': 'proxy.example.test'}`.
- explicit `--config` with `embedding_provider: openai` returned exit `0` with `config_source=explicit`, `config_trust_mode=auto`, no untrusted warning codes, and `remote_embedding={'provider': 'openai', 'host': 'api.openai.com'}`.
Focused verification on 2026-03-26:
- `./.venv/bin/pytest -q -n0 tests/unit/test_config.py::test_load_auto_detected_repo_config_is_untrusted_by_default tests/unit/test_config.py::test_load_explicit_config_is_trusted_in_auto_mode tests/unit/test_config.py::test_load_marks_custom_embedding_endpoints tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_status_json_rejects_invalid_repo_config_trust_env_var tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_status_json_includes_untrusted_repo_config_metadata tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_status_json_marks_explicit_config_as_trusted tests/unit/test_embeddings.py::test_openai_provider_accepts_openrouter_key_and_defaults_base_url tests/unit/test_embeddings.py::test_openai_provider_rejects_custom_base_url_without_opt_in tests/unit/test_embeddings.py::test_openai_provider_uses_openrouter_base_url_override` -> `9 passed in 2.36s`

title: Repo-config trust classification and adapter gating
description:
The shipped contract is narrower than the original title suggests. Current code classifies repo-local config provenance, emits high-signal warnings when auto-discovered repo config requests local adapter overrides, and surfaces that provenance in JSON diagnostics. It does not hard-block adapter loading outright.

The main behavior lives in `src/gloggur/config.py` and `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`. `GloggurConfig.load(...)` distinguishes `explicit`, `auto_discovered`, `repo_dotenv`, and `defaults` sources, records `config_trust_mode`, and computes stable warning codes. In practice, auto-discovered repo config and repo-local dotenv config are treated as untrusted by default in `auto` mode, and any repo-local `adapters:` mapping with a non-empty `module:callable` override adds `untrusted_adapter_override_requested` alongside `untrusted_repo_config`.

The CLI side then threads that metadata into machine-readable payloads instead of silently trusting the repo config. `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` fails closed on malformed `GLOGGUR_REPO_CONFIG_TRUST`, and status-style JSON payloads include `config_source`, `config_trust_mode`, and `security_warning_codes` so an operator can see whether an active adapter override came from trusted operator intent or from an auto-discovered repo file.

The important boundary is that runtime adapter resolution still remains available. The adapter/factory paths in `src/gloggur/adapters/registry.py`, `src/gloggur/parsers/registry.py`, `src/gloggur/embeddings/factory.py`, `src/gloggur/storage/backends.py`, `src/gloggur/runtime/hosts.py`, `src/gloggur/indexer/indexer.py`, and `src/gloggur/watch/service.py` still consume `config.adapters` normally. So the shipped policy is provenance classification plus warning metadata around repo-local adapter overrides, not blanket adapter-disable gating.
design:
- `src/gloggur/config.py`
  - `GloggurConfig.load(...)` establishes config provenance (`explicit`, `auto_discovered`, `repo_dotenv`, `defaults`) and records `config_source_path` / `config_trust_mode`.
  - `_compute_security_warning_codes(...)` is the core trust-classification path. It adds `untrusted_repo_config` for auto-discovered repo config in `auto` mode, and adds `untrusted_adapter_override_requested` when repo-local config contains `adapters.<category>.<adapter_id>: module:callable` overrides.
  - explicit operator config remains trusted in the default `auto` mode unless the operator forces `trust_mode="untrusted"`.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`
  - `_resolve_repo_config_trust_mode()` fails closed on invalid env values with `repo_config_trust_env_invalid`.
  - `_decorate_payload_with_security_metadata(...)` injects `config_source`, `config_trust_mode`, and `security_warning_codes` into JSON payloads and nested `summary` / `debug` sections.
- Actual adapter override resolution remains unchanged and still accepts config-provided overrides:
  - `src/gloggur/adapters/registry.py` loads `module:callable` override factories.
  - `src/gloggur/parsers/registry.py`, `src/gloggur/embeddings/factory.py`, `src/gloggur/storage/backends.py`, `src/gloggur/runtime/hosts.py`, `src/gloggur/indexer/indexer.py`, and `src/gloggur/watch/service.py` all thread `config.adapters` into adapter resolution.
- Public operator surface:
  - `docs/ADAPTERS.md` still documents repo-config `adapters:` overrides as the extension mechanism.
  - `README.md`, `docs/QUICKSTART.md`, and `docs/ERROR_CODES.md` document the warning/error surface around repo-local config trust.

Important scope clarification:
- the shipped feature is trust classification plus warning/reporting around adapter overrides;
- it is not a hard deny path that prevents adapter overrides from loading solely because the config was auto-discovered.
acceptance_criteria:
Auto-discovered repo config that contains local adapter overrides emits machine-readable warning metadata including `untrusted_repo_config` and `untrusted_adapter_override_requested`.
Explicit `--config` paths remain trusted by default in `auto` mode and therefore do not emit the untrusted adapter warning unless the operator forces `GLOGGUR_REPO_CONFIG_TRUST=untrusted` / `trust_mode="untrusted"`.
JSON CLI payloads expose `config_source`, `config_trust_mode`, and `security_warning_codes` so operators can see config provenance instead of silently trusting repo-local adapter settings.
Invalid `GLOGGUR_REPO_CONFIG_TRUST` values fail closed with `repo_config_trust_env_invalid`.
Adapter override resolution paths remain operational and continue to consume `config.adapters`; the shipped contract is warning/reporting, not blanket runtime blocking.
notes:
Landed in commit `5c90c04` (`feat: harden mixed-trust config, artifact, and release flows`).
Direct probe on 2026-03-26:
- auto-discovered `.gloggur.yaml` with `adapters.runtime.python_local: attacker:factory` produced `AUTO_LOAD auto_discovered auto ['untrusted_adapter_override_requested', 'untrusted_repo_config']`
- explicit `--config` load of the same file produced `EXPLICIT_LOAD explicit auto []`
- forcing `trust_mode='untrusted'` for the same explicit file produced `FORCED_UNTRUSTED_LOAD explicit untrusted ['untrusted_adapter_override_requested', 'untrusted_repo_config']`
- `status --json` in the auto-discovered case returned exit `0` with `config_source=auto_discovered`, `config_trust_mode=auto`, and `security_warning_codes=['untrusted_adapter_override_requested', 'untrusted_repo_config']`
Focused verification on 2026-03-26:
- `./.venv/bin/pytest -q -n0 tests/unit/test_config.py::test_load_auto_detected_repo_config_is_untrusted_by_default tests/unit/test_config.py::test_load_explicit_config_is_trusted_in_auto_mode tests/unit/test_config.py::test_load_explicit_config_can_be_forced_untrusted tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_status_json_rejects_invalid_repo_config_trust_env_var tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_status_json_includes_untrusted_repo_config_metadata tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_status_json_marks_explicit_config_as_trusted` -> `6 passed in 2.34s`
Gap between the original low-signal wording and current shipped behavior:
- trust classification and JSON surfacing are implemented today
- adapter overrides are warned on when repo-local/untrusted, but not actually disabled
- the remaining missing piece, if desired in future, would be a true deny/allow policy layered on top of the current warning metadata

title: Mixed-trust repo hardening
description: Security hardening initiative for Gloggur based on the repository-grounded threat model. Track staged rollout work for repo-config trust classification, adapter/provider gating, artifact provenance, support bundle sensitivity, provider/model availability bounds, and release provenance hardening.
design: Epic for mixed-trust hardening. Keep implementation in child issues covering repo-config provenance and adapter warnings, remote provider endpoint controls, artifact provenance and restore validation, support bundle sensitivity metadata, release provenance, and remaining local embedding bootstrap failure behavior. The parent tracks rollout completeness and threat-model alignment; child issues own code, docs, tests, and closure evidence.
acceptance_criteria:
- Child issues document the shipped trust boundary and any intentionally non-enforced warning-only behavior
- Remaining open child work is limited to the local embedding bootstrap failure path or explicitly deferred with rationale
- Final epic closure confirms docs, JSON diagnostics, error codes, and regression tests cover the mixed-trust boundaries
- No future work is hidden in this parent without a concrete child issue
notes: Audited during /audit-beads-quality on 2026-05-03. The epic has useful scope and child issues, but lacked design, acceptance criteria, and estimate. Five child issues are closed with evidence; bd-qb9 remains in progress for bounded offline local-model bootstrap behavior.

title: Fix regressions from identifier-first search tightening
description: Current code shows this issue was the immediate regression-cleanup pass after the identifier-first / compact-output search landing, not another search-routing feature. The concrete shipped boundary is: `search --json` still maps metadata-store connection failures to a structured `io_failure` envelope instead of leaking raw sqlite errors or top-level tracebacks; the search surface remains on ContextPack v2 after the tightening work; and the touched CLI/runtime files stay inside the shared static-quality verification lane. The original mention of an inspect/docstring warning is best understood as part of that verification cleanup on `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`, not as a separate user-facing search contract change.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`
  - `_with_io_failure_handling(...)` is the top-level fail-closed wrapper for CLI commands.
  - the `search` command still routes metadata-store open failures into the stable structured error family rather than ad hoc exception text.
  - the command also preserves the ContextPack v2 success contract after the identifier-first tightening work.
- `src/gloggur/io_failures.py`
  - defines the structured `io_failure` payload family used by CLI command wrappers.
- `scripts/run_static_quality_gates.py`
  - still defines the shared Ruff/Mypy/Black verification surface for the runtime package and control-plane files.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py` and `tests/integration/test_run_static_quality_gates_harness.py`
  - pin the structured metadata-store failure envelope and the static-gate stage contract.

Scope clarification:
- this issue repaired verification and failure-contract regressions introduced by the identifier-first change set;
- it did not materially redefine the routing behavior already tracked by `bd-d9j`.
acceptance_criteria:
- `search --json` maps metadata-store connection failures into a structured `io_failure` payload with stable category/operation/path fields.
- The ContextPack v2 success contract remains intact after the cleanup.
- The static quality gate still covers the touched runtime/verification files with deterministic Ruff/Mypy/Black stages.
- No new routing or payload drift is introduced while restoring the failure and verification contracts.
notes:
Current evidence on 2026-03-26:
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_search_json_wraps_metadata_store_connect_failure` still proves the structured `io_failure` envelope for metadata-store sqlite open failures.
- `scripts/run_static_quality_gates.py` still defines the three-stage shared verification lane over control-plane files plus `src/gloggur`.
Focused verification on 2026-03-26:
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_search_json_wraps_metadata_store_connect_failure`
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main.py::test_search_json_contract_remains_contextpack_v2_after_find_addition`
- `tests/integration/test_run_static_quality_gates_harness.py::test_run_static_quality_gates_harness_succeeds_for_verification_surface`
- included in the focused pytest batch -> `20 passed in 3.79s`

title: Identifier-first search tightening and compact agent output
description: Current code shows this landed as a search-contract tightening pass rather than just a ranking tweak. Query hints now drive more identifier/declaration-first behavior in routed search, the semantic backend remains lazy until the chosen mode or routing outcome actually needs it, and `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` exposes a compact agent-oriented JSON mode that trims hit/snippet volume and summary metadata without changing the underlying decision semantics. The shipped scope also includes clearer next-action metadata (`open_hit_1`, `narrow_by_path`, `refine_query`) and benchmark-style regression anchors for code queries like `escape_leading_slashes`, `after_request order`, and `url_has_allowed_host_and_scheme`. This issue is therefore best understood as the landing of the identifier-first / compact-output search surface that later definition-first follow-ups refined further.
design:
Implementation touchpoints in the current codebase:
- `src/gloggur/search/router/hints.py` and `src/gloggur/search/router/engine.py`
  - query-kind/domain hints steer backend selection and exact/fixed-string behavior for identifier and declaration lookups.
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py`
  - `_apply_search_compact_mode(...)` defines the compact agent JSON surface.
  - `search --json --debug-router` normalizes debug payloads without eagerly constructing the semantic provider.
- `src/gloggur/search/hybrid_search.py`
  - legacy callers still receive explicit `search_mode`, `ranking_mode`, and warning metadata rather than a hidden behavior change.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py`
  - pins compact-mode output trimming and the lazy debug-router path.
- `tests/unit/test_search_router.py`
  - pins declaration queries that can route decisively to the symbol backend.

Scope clarification:
- this issue is broader than compact formatting alone;
- it is the search-surface landing that combined routing bias, lazy semantic init, and agent-oriented output trimming.
acceptance_criteria:
- Declaration and identifier-style searches can route through exact/symbol-first behavior without forcing immediate semantic initialization.
- `search --json --compact` trims hit count, snippet length, and summary metadata while preserving decisive status, query kind, and next action.
- The debug-router path does not eagerly initialize the semantic provider when the router result can be produced without it.
- The public search contract remains `contextpack_v2` even when compact mode is used.
- Regression coverage anchors benchmark-style code queries on the tightened search surface.
notes:
Current evidence on 2026-03-26:
- `src/gloggur/cli/main.py` still exposes `_apply_search_compact_mode(...)` and keeps `search` on `contract_version="contextpack_v2"`.
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py` still proves the debug-router path keeps semantic init lazy.
- `tests/unit/test_search_router.py` still proves declaration queries can choose the symbol backend decisively.
Focused verification on 2026-03-26:
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py::test_search_json_debug_router_normalizes_debug_payload_without_eager_semantic_init`
- `tests/unit/test_cli_main_command_families.py::test_search_json_compact_mode_trims_hits_and_summary_metadata`
- `tests/unit/test_search_router.py::test_search_router_declaration_queries_can_choose_symbol_backend`
- included in the focused pytest batch -> `30 passed in 8.84s`

title: Fix docstring and Ruff regressions from language-support rollout
description:
Follow-up bugfix for post-rollout verification failures:
- inspect hotspot docstring test reports Missing docstring symbols in src/gloggur/cli/main.py due to nested helper functions added for skipped-extension diagnostics without docstrings.
- static quality gates fail at Ruff stage due to E501 line-length violations introduced in src/gloggur/cli/main.py and src/gloggur/parsers/support_contract.py.

Acceptance:
- Add docstrings to flagged nested helpers.
- Reformat long lines to satisfy Ruff in gated files.
- Re-run the two failing integration tests and confirm pass.
notes:
Implemented follow-up regressions fix:
- Added docstrings to nested skipped-extension _record helpers in src/gloggur/cli/main.py so inspect hotspot docstring audit stays clean.
- Wrapped long lines in src/gloggur/cli/main.py and formatted src/gloggur/parsers/support_contract.py to satisfy static quality black/ruff gates.

Verification:
- source .venv/bin/activate && pytest -n 0 'tests/integration/test_cli.py::test_cli_inspect_reports_no_missing_docstrings_for_recent_f11_hotspots[src/gloggur/cli/main.py]' tests/integration/test_run_static_quality_gates_harness.py::test_run_static_quality_gates_harness_succeeds_for_verification_surface -q
